Every workflow that operates on Integration Packages also supports Partner Directory entries — selected via a single mode input.

What's New in v1.3.0

Version 1.3.0 is the largest release to date.

The Dashboard — See Your Entire Landscape at a Glance

The headline feature is the Integration Suite Operations Dashboard: a static HTML page, automatically generated by a scheduled workflow and published to GitHub Pages. It shows every integration package and every IFlow across all configured environments — runtime status, deployed version, version drift between stages, and whether the package is managed by the pipeline. Color-coded health indicators make problem areas immediately visible.

The dashboard gives an immediate overview of every integration package across all environments — version drift, deployment errors, and unmanaged packages are visible at a glance.

Expanding a package reveals the full detail. Two typical situations a team encounters during release work:

Version drift — both IFlows are running a newer version on DEV and TST than on PRD. Typical during an active release: the new version has been promoted to test but not yet to production. Missing deployment — the second IFlow is configured for production but shows — in the PRD runtime column: it has not been imported yet. The deployment configuration says it should be there; BTP says it is not. Without this dashboard, that gap would likely go unnoticed until a runtime error surfaces.

The dashboard is regenerated daily and can be triggered manually at any time. Self-contained HTML — no server required.

GitHub App Authentication

v1.3.0 replaces Personal Access Tokens with GitHub App authentication. Two dedicated apps handle the read and write sides of the pipeline with minimal, explicit permissions. Token rotation is automatic.

Access Policies as Code

Access Policies can now be version-controlled and promoted through the pipeline. The pattern is identical to integration packages: download from DEV, commit to Git, review, deploy.

Automatic Log Level Management

The deployment pipeline now automatically sets each iFlow's log level to the configured operational level after deployment. No manual navigation to the operations cockpit after every release.

A Different Approach to Integration Lifecycle Management

 
SAP provides transport and lifecycle management capabilities for Integration Suite. This solution takes a different path: Git becomes the system of record, GitHub Actions drives the entire lifecycle, and every operation — download, deploy, delete, configure — is automated and auditable. For teams that have adopted a DevOps mindset and want full control over their integration pipeline, this is a deliberate alternative rather than an add-on.

Built Together, Open Sourced Together

This solution was developed collaboratively by SAP and Mercedes-Benz to reduce the manual overhead of release operations on a large enterprise integration landscape. It is available to any team, free of charge, under the Apache 2.0 license, and works on both GitHub Enterprise Cloud and GitHub Enterprise Server.

What's Coming in This Series

  1. The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline — what actually happens, step by step
  2. Managing parameters and log levels — environment-specific configuration as code
  3. The Release Import workflow — controlled promotion using Git tags and branches
  4. Access Policies as code — governance and auditability
  5. The Dashboard in depth — how to read it, configure it, and act on what it shows
  6. Extending the pipeline with Customer Exits — inject your own tests and deployment rules without touching the core

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

entry.title: Cloud Integration - Attachment names missing using the XI Sender Adapter
entry.content:
During the migration of interfaces from PI/PO to the Integration Suite, there were XI Protocol-based interfaces that sent attachments. The attachments were supposed to be saved as files on an SFTP server in the target system under their original names. The migration created an iFlow with an XI sender. Unfortunately, the original names of the attachments were lost. A solution has been implemented to save these names and use them when storing files on the SFTP server.

What's the problem?

An ABAP proxy can send not only the main payload but also attachments to the XI sender adapter. The attachments in the XI message are appended to the message by the sender adapter.

The following simple example scenario has been developed to illustrate this point. A message containing attachments is sent to Cloud Integration via an ABAP proxy. This message is received by a generic XI sender iFlow, which forwards it to an interface-specific iFlow via ProcessDirect, without losing the attachments or their original names. The specific iFlow should then store the main payload, along with all the attachments, under their original names on an SFTP server.

ABAP Proxy Message Monitor

The sample request shows that the attachments are Readme.txt and TestDocument.pdf. The received iFlow uses a Groovy script to log all the message's attachments, along with their names, in the MPL.

XI Sender iFlow - log attachments

def Message logAttachmentList(Message message) {
    // Log attachment infos with original names
    def attachments = message.getAttachments()
    if (attachments == null || attachments.isEmpty()) return message // No attachments
    def line = "----------------------------------------------------------\n"
    def attLog = ""
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    if (messageLog != null){
        attLog += "AttachmentCount: " + attachments?.size()?.toString() + "\n" + line
        attachments.eachWithIndex { att, index ->
            attLog += "Attachment: " + att?.key + "\n"
            def attachment = att?.value
            attLog += "ContentType: " + attachment?.getContentType() + "\n"
            attLog += "Name: " + attachment?.getName() + "\n" + line
        }
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Attachmentlist.txt", attLog, "text/plain; charset=UTF-8")
    }
    return message
}

Unfortunately, the original names of the attachments are lost in the process, meaning they can no longer be read directly from the attachment object using the getName() method.

AttachmentCount: 2
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
ContentType: text/plain
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
ContentType: application/pdf
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
----------------------------------------------------------

How to solve the issue?

The message trace shows that the XI sender adapter stores the attachment reference as its name and the manifest content of the XI message in the SAP_XiManifest property. Both the reference and the original name are included here.

Property SAP_XiManifest

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?>
<Manifest wsu:Id="wsuid-manifest-5CABE13F5C59AB7FE10000000A1551F7" xmlns="http://sap.com/xi/XI/Message/30" xmlns:wsu="http://www.docs.oasis-open.org/wss/2004/01/oasis-200401-wss-wssecurity-utility-1.0.xsd" xmlns:ns2="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">
    <Payload ns2:href="cid:payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com">
        <Name>Readme.txt</Name>
        <Description/>
        <Type>ApplicationAttachment</Type>
    </Payload>
    <Payload ns2:href="cid:payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com">
        <Name>TestDocument.pdf</Name>
        <Description/>
        <Type>ApplicationAttachment</Type>
    </Payload>
    <Payload ns2:href="cid:payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CC947@sap.com">
        <Name>MainDocument</Name>
        <Description/>
        <Type>Application</Type>
    </Payload>
</Manifest>


To solve this problem, a second Groovy script is added to preserve the original names. This script analyses the manifest and stores the references as key-value pairs in a map, using the reference as the key and the original name as the value. In order for the map to be passed as a header with the name XI_AttachmentList to the next iFlow, it is converted into JSON format.

XI Sender iFlow

def Message processData(Message message) {
    // Get SAP_XiManifest property
    def manifestXml = message.getProperty('SAP_XiManifest')
    if (manifestXml == null || manifestXml.trim().isEmpty()) {
        // No manifest found, return message as-is
        return message
    }
    
    // Parse the XML and create HashMap to store attachment names with their content IDs as keys
    def manifest = new XmlSlurper().parseText(manifestXml)
    def attachmentMap = new HashMap<String, String>()
    
    // Find all Payload elements where Type == "ApplicationAttachment",
    // Get the name and the href attribute and remove the "cid:" prefix
    manifest.Payload.each { payload ->
        if (payload.Type.text() == 'ApplicationAttachment') {
            def name = payload.Name.text()
            def attributes = payload.attributes()
            def hrefAttr = null
            attributes.each { key, value ->
                if (key.toString().contains('href')) {
                    hrefAttr = value
                }
            }
            
            if (hrefAttr && hrefAttr.startsWith('cid:')) {
                def contentId = hrefAttr.substring(4) // Remove "cid:" prefix
                attachmentMap[contentId] = name
            }
        }
    }
    
    // Convert HashMap to JSON string and store in a header
    message.setHeader('XI_AttachmentList', JsonOutput.toJson(attachmentMap))
    return message
}


Header XI_AttachmentList

Header XI_AttachmentList

{
    "payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com": "TestDocument.pdf",
    "payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com": "Readme.txt"
}

The second iFlow must allow the header XI_AttachmentList in the runtime configuration. As in the previous iFlow, all attachments are logged in the MPL along with their names using a Groovy script. However, the extended script takes into account the header variable XI_AttachmentList and maps the attachment name to its original name.

def Message logAttachmentListWithNames(Message message) {
    // Log attachment infos with original names
    def jsonAttList = message.getHeader('XI_AttachmentList', String)
    def attachmentNames = [:]
    if (jsonAttList != null) attachmentNames = new JsonSlurper()?.parseText(jsonAttList)
    def attachments = message.getAttachments()
    if (attachments == null || attachments.isEmpty()) attachments = message.getProperty('AttachmentsMap')
    if (attachments == null || attachments.isEmpty()) return message // No attachments
    def line = "----------------------------------------------------------\n"
    def attLog = ""
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    if (messageLog != null){
        attLog += "AttachmentCount: " + attachments?.size()?.toString() + "\n"
        attLog += "AttachmentNames:\n"
        attachmentNames.eachWithIndex { attNam, index ->
            attLog += attNam?.key + " = " + attNam?.value + "\n"
        }
        attLog += line
        attachments.eachWithIndex { att, index ->
            attLog += "Attachment: " + att?.key + "\n"
            def attachment = att?.value
            attLog += "ContentType: " + attachment?.getContentType() + "\n"
            attLog += "Name: " + attachment?.getName() + "\n"
            if (jsonAttList != null) attLog += "TranslatedName: " + (attachmentNames[att?.key]?:attachment?.getName()) + "\n" + line
        }
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Attachmentlist.txt", attLog, "text/plain; charset=UTF-8")
    }
    return message
}

 

AttachmentCount: 2
AttachmentNames:
payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com = TestDocument.pdf
payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com = Readme.txt
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
ContentType: text/plain
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
TranslatedName: Readme.txt
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
ContentType: application/pdf
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
TranslatedName: TestDocument.pdf
----------------------------------------------------------

To simplify processing all the attachments, a Groovy script reads them into a property as a map. In addition, the JSON content from the XI_AttachmentList header is converted into a map and stored in the AttachmentNames property.

def Message saveAttachments(Message message) {
    // Store attachments in property
    def attachments = new HashMap<String, DataHandler>(message.getAttachments())
    message.setProperty('AttachmentsMap', attachments)
    // Store the number of attachments in property
    message.setProperty('AttachmentCount', attachments.size())
    // Clear the attachments
    message.getAttachments().clear()
    message.getAttachmentWrapperObjects().clear()
    // Get original attachment names and store as hashmap in property
    def jsonAttList = message.getHeader('XI_AttachmentList', String)
    def attachmentNames = [:]
    if (jsonAttList != null) attachmentNames = new JsonSlurper()?.parseText(jsonAttList)
    message.setProperty('AttachmentNames', attachmentNames)
    return message
}

All attachments are then processed using a Looping Process Call.

Receiver iFlow - Transfer attachments to an SFTP server

In this Local Integration Process, the current attachment in the loop is read and its contents are written to the message body. The AttachmentNames property is then used to determine the original file name, which is stored in the SFTP_FileName property. The Local Integration Process responsible for transferring the data to the SFTP server can then retrieve the file name from this property.

def Message getNextAttachment(Message message) {
    def attachments = message.getProperty('AttachmentsMap')
    if (attachments.isEmpty()) {
        message.setProperty('AttachmentCount', 0)
        return message
    }
    // Get next attachment to be processed
    def nextAttch = attachments.keySet().iterator().next()
    // Remove the attachment from the Map
    def attachment = attachments.remove(nextAttch)
    //def attachmentName = attachment?.getName()?:"attachment_"+attachments.size()
    def attachmentNames = message.getProperty('AttachmentNames')
    def attachmentName = attachmentNames[attachment?.getName()?:"attachment_"+attachments.size()]?:attachment?.getName()?:"attachment_"+attachments.size()
    
    // Replace the message with attachment
    message.setBody(attachment.getContent())
    // Update the AttachmentCount
    message.setProperty('AttachmentCount', attachments.size())
    message.setProperty('SFTP_FileName', attachmentName)
    return message
}

 

Conclusion

The XI Sender Adapter provides all the necessary information in the form of an XML manifest. Using this information alongside a short Groovy script enables us to easily save and reuse the original names of the attachments.

Useful links XI Sender Adapter:

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/configure-xi-sender-adapter 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/cloud-integration-configuring-scenario-using-the-xi-sender-adapter/ba-p/13350089

entry.title: CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite / The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline
entry.content:
The first post in this series introduced CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite: what it is, why it exists, and what it looks like from the outside. This post goes inside the machine.

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow is the beating heart of the pipeline. It is the first workflow most teams run when they adopt the solution, and it is the one they use every time a developer changes something on DEV and wants to move it forward. Understanding exactly what happens in each step — and why those steps are designed the way they are — is the foundation for understanding everything else in the system.

  • The Problem It Solves
  • The Git Branch Model
  • What You Fill In
  • Inside the Pipeline
    • Authorization and Environment Resolution
    • Download and Commit to Git
    • Partner Directory in Git: Decoded and Readable
    • Clean Slate Before Deployment
    • Upload and Activate
  • What the Pipeline Does Not Do
  • Key Takeaways

The Problem It Solves

In a typical SAP Integration Suite landscape, a developer makes changes on the development tenant. Those changes live in the SAP Integration Suite design time. When it is time to promote them, someone has to manually export the package, import it into the test system, apply the right configuration values, and deploy each artifact — Message Mappings, Script Collections, Value Mappings, and iFlows. If anything was already deployed in test, it might need to be cleaned up first. Parameters might differ between systems. Log levels need attention. And none of it is recorded anywhere in a way that supports auditability or rollback.

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow replaces all of that with a single button press that is fully automated, fully recorded, and repeatable.

The Git Branch Model

Before walking through the pipeline, it helps to understand how Git is structured in this solution. There are two permanent branches with distinct roles.

The development branch is where all active work lives. Every package download, every configuration change, every new artifact lands here first. This is the branch the Download → Git → Deploy workflow writes to, and it is the branch the deployment jobs read from when uploading to TST.

There is no option to select a different target branch when triggering the workflow. The development branch is hardcoded as the destination. This is a deliberate design choice: developers should not have to think about Git branches at all during the day-to-day download and deploy cycle. The branch model is an infrastructure concern, not a developer concern. As landscape scenarios grow more complex — multiple parallel development streams, for example — this may evolve, but for the common case the simplicity is intentional.

The main branch represents what has been formally released. As long as work is progressing on development, main is untouched. The merge from development into main happens as part of the Release Import process — which creates a Git tag, a GitHub Release, and the clean reference point used for controlled promotion to production. That process is covered in a later post in this series.

What You Fill In

The workflow is (Dev) BTP Download to GIT and Upload/Deploy to TST, triggered manually via workflow_dispatch. The team member fills in three fields:

  • ID — the Integration Package ID or Partner Directory partner ID to process
  • Mode — IntegrationPackages or PartnerDirectory
  • JIRA Story / SNOW Incident — a reference that becomes the Git commit message, linking every change in the repository to a traceable work item

One important note on the ID field: the pipeline always operates on complete entities as its smallest unit. In IntegrationPackages mode, every operation applies to the entire Integration Package — there is no option to download or deploy a single iFlow or other individual artifact. In PartnerDirectory mode, the same principle applies: one complete Partner Directory entry per ID. This applies to all workflows in this CICD solution, not just this one.

All artifacts must be versioned — before triggering the workflow, every artifact in the package must have a saved version in SAP Integration Suite. Draft artifacts are not supported and will cause the download to fail. This is a standard practice in any version-controlled pipeline: only content that has been deliberately saved as a version is considered ready to leave the development tenant.

A note on Package IDs — if the ID in the screenshot looks unusual (KIhovby8X0TS8uXS3Oo1CCAcu), that is intentional. When creating a package in SAP Integration Suite, the ID is a free-text field you set once at creation time. Unlike the package name, it can never be changed afterwards. Using a GUID avoids any confusion down the line: names get renamed, reorganised, or reused — a GUID stays unambiguous forever.

Three fields, one button. The ID and Mode select what to process; the JIRA or SNOW reference becomes the Git commit message, tying every repository change to a traceable work item.

Inside the Pipeline

That is it for the inputs. Everything else is automated. The screenshot below shows a completed run for an integration package — 1m 34s from button press to a fully deployed package on TST. The actual runtime depends on the number of artifacts in the package: the deployment phase is typically the longest part of the process, as all artifacts are activated — Message Mappings, Script Collections, and Value Mappings are always deployed automatically, while iFlows are deployed based on their individual deployment configuration. The sections below explain what each phase does and why it is designed the way it is.

 

Authorization and Environment Resolution

Before anything touches SAP Integration Suite, the workflow verifies that the person who triggered it is a member of the authorized GitHub team. The team slug is defined in the BTP_DEV_TEAM_SLUG repository variable, configured once during the initial repository setup. If the check fails, the run stops immediately — no SAP Integration Suite credentials are used, no API calls are made. This applies to both modes.

Authorization is not just a security gate. It is also where the workflow binds to the DEV GitHub Environment — a named set of secrets and variables scoped to a specific job — and resolves all the connection details — BTP API URL, token endpoint, technical user, and service key identity — that every subsequent step will use. Before the deployment phase, the workflow binds to the TST GitHub Environment in the same way — resolving its own set of credentials and API endpoints. The team membership check is not repeated: it runs once, at the start of the workflow, and covers the entire run. No URLs or credentials are hardcoded anywhere in the workflow. Everything is environment-driven, which means the same workflow definition works across any landscape configuration without modification — adding a stage is a matter of adding a GitHub Environment with the appropriate credentials, not changing the workflow itself.

Download and Commit to Git

Once authorization is confirmed and the DEV environment is resolved, the pipeline checks out the development branch and removes the existing content for the package or Partner Directory ID. This clean-first approach ensures that if an artifact was deleted on DEV, it does not silently survive in the repository. The pipeline then authenticates with SAP Integration Suite, fetches the new content, and commits it — what lands in Git is an exact reflection of what is in SAP Integration Suite at that moment.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the package is fetched as a ZIP archive. The extraction goes two levels deep: the outer package ZIP is unpacked, and each artifact archive inside it is unpacked as well. The result is that real source files — iFlow configurations, Groovy scripts, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, Script Collections — land as individual readable files in Git. Every change to a Groovy script or a mapping is visible in the commit diff and reviewable in a pull request. It also opens the door for automated code scans — static analysis of Groovy scripts for security or quality requirements — as part of custom pipeline enhancements. And because the source files are plain text, the repository's built-in search becomes a lightweight where-used tool: searching across all packages for a specific endpoint URL, a function name, or a configuration value instantly shows every iFlow and script that references it.

For PartnerDirectory mode, the four object types are retrieved individually via API calls and written as four JSON files, one per type: String Parameters, Binary Parameters, Alternative Partners, and Authorized Users.

Regardless of mode, the commit uses the JIRA or SNOW reference entered at dispatch as the commit message. Every change in the repository is therefore traceable to a work item. Configuration and deployment control files for each iFlow are also created or updated as part of the commit — the detail on these is covered in the next post in this series. Crucially, these files are separate from the artifact content that is deleted and re-fetched on each download; any stage-specific parameter values or deployment settings the team has configured are preserved across repeated runs, not overwritten.

Partner Directory in Git: Decoded and Readable

The way Binary Parameters are stored in Git deserves a special mention. In the Partner Directory of SAP Integration Suite, Binary Parameter values are base64-encoded blobs — not human-readable in the portal. The pipeline stores them in two parts. The index file BinaryParameters.json lists each entry with its metadata and a filename reference instead of the raw encoded value. The actual content is decoded and written as a separate file under a Binary/ subfolder, named after the entry.

 

The Value field holds a filename reference, not a base64 blob. The decoded content lives as a separate file in the Binary/ subfolder.

The result is that an XSD schema used for Receiver Determination, or any other binary payload, is stored as its actual readable content in the repository. The example below is a simplified illustration — in practice this file would contain a full XSD definition.

 

The decoded file as it appears in Git. Every change to the content is visible in the commit diff and reviewable in a pull request — something simply not possible with an opaque encoded string.

Clean Slate Before Deployment

Before uploading the new version to TST, the pipeline clears what is already there. This happens in two layers, and the order matters.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the runtime artifacts are undeployed first. The order matters: if the design-time package were deleted while artifacts are still active in the runtime, a runtime restart would leave those artifacts unable to find their definitions — or worse, leave orphaned objects in the runtime that no longer have any design-time counterpart and that nobody remembers deploying. Cleaning the runtime first avoids that risk. Only once the runtime is clear is the design-time package deleted.

For PartnerDirectory mode, there is no runtime deployment to stop. The existing Partner Directory entries are deleted directly from the design time.

In both cases, clearing the design time completely before uploading is what makes the clean-slate guarantee possible. If an artifact was removed from the package on DEV, it will not survive on TST — there is no mechanism by which a deleted artifact can linger in the target environment. Without this step, TST would gradually accumulate ghost artifacts that no longer exist on DEV, making it an unreliable mirror of the source.

Upload and Activate

With the target tenant clean, the pipeline uploads the new content from the development Git branch and activates it.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the package source is reassembled into a ZIP — the format the SAP Integration Suite public API expects for package upload — and uploaded to the TST design time. The deployment engine then activates the artifacts in the package: Message Mappings, Script Collections, and Value Mappings are always deployed automatically. Integration Flows are deployed selectively — each iFlow has a deployment flag in its configuration file committed to Git, and only those marked for deployment in the target environment are activated. Since the package was deleted and re-uploaded, every deployed iFlow is new to the runtime and starts with the default log level of INFO. After deployment, the pipeline updates each iFlow's log level to whatever is configured for that environment in Git. No one needs to visit the operations cockpit to check or adjust anything afterward.

For PartnerDirectory mode, the pipeline creates the Partner Directory entries on the TST Cloud Integration runtime. Support for Edge Integration Cell runtimes is planned and will be added once the required public APIs become available.

What the Pipeline Does Not Do

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow intentionally stops at TST. It does not automatically promote to production. Moving to PRD — or to any further stage — is a deliberate, separate action. The Release Import workflow handles that step, using Git tags and branch comparisons to calculate exactly what has changed since the last release and deploy only the delta. That is the subject of a later post in this series.

Key Takeaways

  • The pipeline operates on complete entities as its smallest unit: one full Integration Package in IntegrationPackages mode, one complete Partner Directory entry in PartnerDirectory mode. Individual iFlows or artifacts cannot be targeted in isolation — this applies to all workflows in the solution.
  • A single workflow dispatch downloads a package from DEV, commits it to Git with a traceable reference, and deploys it to TST — fully automated, no manual handover.
  • The download creates and maintains one configuration file per package containing the parameter values for all iFlows in that package, and one deployment control file per package covering all iFlows — all committed alongside the package source in Git. Stage-specific values set by the team are preserved across repeated downloads. The detail is covered in the next post.
  • The clean-before-upload pattern ensures the target environment reflects Git exactly, including deletions.
  • Partner Directory is fully supported by the same pipeline. Switching the mode input is all it takes — the same authorization, Git commit, and deployment steps apply, with Partner Directory-specific handling where the content differs.
  • Every action that happens — every download, every parameter value, every deployment decision — is recorded in Git history.

The next post in this series covers the configuration layer in depth: how externalized parameters and log levels are managed per environment, how to add a new stage, and how to update a parameter value without triggering a full download.

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

entry.title: Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator
entry.content:
Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator

Introduction

In SAP integration projects, technical design documents are critical for capturing interface details, design decisions, mapping rules, adapter configurations, error handling, and operational monitoring. However, creating these documents manually for every SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CPI, interface can be time-consuming and repetitive.

This blog explains a practical use case for building an AI-powered application that can generate SAP CPI technical design documents automatically using inputs such as manual interface details, iFlow packages, and mapping sheets.

The goal is not to replace solution architects or developers, but to accelerate documentation creation and provide a consistent first draft that can be reviewed, edited, and approved by the project team.

Business Problem

SAP CPI developers often spend significant effort documenting integration flows after development is complete. A typical technical design document includes:

  • Interface overview
  • Source and target system details
  • Adapter configuration
  • Mapping and transformation logic
  • Error handling
  • Retry and reprocessing strategy
  • Monitoring and logging
  • Security and authentication
  • Testing scope
  • Deployment checklist

In large integration programs, the same documentation structure is repeated across many interfaces. This creates an opportunity to use AI to generate a structured draft based on available technical inputs.

Proposed Solution

The proposed solution is an AI-assisted SAP CPI TDD Generator.

The application allows users to enter basic interface details, upload an SAP CPI iFlow package, optionally attach a mapping sheet, and generate a technical design document in Word format.

The generated document can include:

  1. Technical design document content
  2. Architecture diagram
  3. Mapping sheet appendix
  4. Test scenarios
  5. Deployment checklist
  6. Assumptions and dependencies

The user can review and edit the generated output before downloading the final document.

High-Level Architecture

The application consists of the following components:

 

Key Features

  1. Manual TDD Generation

The user can provide basic information such as:

  • Interface name
  • Source system
  • Target system
  • Sender adapter
  • Receiver adapter
  • Mapping logic
  • Error handling
  • Integration flow description

The AI model uses this information to generate a structured technical design document.

  1. TDD Generation Using iFlow Package

The application can accept an exported SAP CPI iFlow package in ZIP format. The backend extracts relevant files such as:

  • .iflw
  • XML files
  • Groovy scripts
  • XSLT mappings
  • WSDL files
  • XSD files
  • Properties files

This extracted information is passed to the AI model to generate a more accurate document.

  1. Mapping Sheet Attachment

The user can upload a mapping sheet in Excel or CSV format. The application reads the mapping data and includes it in the generated Word document as an appendix.

This is useful for documenting source-to-target field-level transformations.

  1. Architecture Diagram Generation

The application generates a basic architecture diagram showing the integration flow:

Source System → Sender Adapter → SAP CPI → Mapping/Transformation → Receiver Adapter → Target System

It also includes monitoring, logging, and error handling as part of the diagram.

  1. Word Document Export

The final output is generated as a Word document. The document includes:

  • Interface details
  • Architecture diagram
  • AI-generated TDD content
  • Mapping sheet appendix

This makes the document easy to review, modify, and share with project stakeholders.

 

Technology Stack

A simple prototype can be built using the following technologies:

Component

Technology

Frontend

Streamlit

Backend

Python

AI Model Access

OpenRouter / OpenAI-compatible API

Document Generation

python-docx

Mapping Sheet Processing

pandas, openpyxl

Diagram Generation

Pillow

File Processing

zipfile

This stack is lightweight and suitable for a quick proof of concept.

Sample User Flow

  1. User opens the Streamlit application.
  2. User selects one of the generation options:
    • Manual Entry
    • Generate using iFlow Package
    • Generate using iFlow Package + Mapping Sheet
  3. User provides interface details.
  4. User uploads the iFlow ZIP file.
  5. User uploads the mapping sheet, if available.
  6. User clicks Generate TDD.
  7. The application extracts technical details.
  8. The AI model generates the TDD content.
  9. The user reviews or edits the generated document.
  10. The user downloads the final Word document.

Prompt Engineering Approach

The application uses a controlled prompt to reduce hallucination. The prompt instructs the AI model to behave as an SAP CPI Integration Architect and generate a technical design document using only the provided information.

Example instruction:

Use the iFlow package details where available.

Use the mapping sheet details where available.

Only use information provided by the user, iFlow package, or mapping sheet.

Do not invent missing information.

If any information is missing, mention "To be confirmed".

Do not include passwords, secrets, API keys, or credentials.

This approach helps ensure that the generated document remains reliable and does not include unsupported assumptions.

Generated TDD Sections

The generated technical design document can include the following sections:

  1. Document Purpose
  2. Interface Overview
  3. Business Requirement Summary
  4. Architecture Diagram Description
  5. Source System Details
  6. Target System Details
  7. SAP CPI iFlow Design
  8. Sender Adapter Configuration
  9. Receiver Adapter Configuration
  10. Message Mapping and Transformation Logic
  11. Routing Logic
  12. Exception Handling
  13. Retry and Reprocessing Strategy
  14. Security and Authentication
  15. Monitoring and Logging
  16. Externalized Parameters
  17. Mapping Sheet Summary
  18. Assumptions
  19. Dependencies
  20. Testing Scope
  21. Test Scenarios
  22. Deployment Checklist
  23. Appendix Reference

Benefits

This solution can help integration teams by:

  • Reducing manual documentation effort
  • Improving consistency across TDD documents
  • Accelerating handover from development to support
  • Creating a reusable documentation framework
  • Helping junior developers understand CPI interface design
  • Reducing missing sections in technical documentation
  • Enabling faster review cycles

Important Considerations

While AI can accelerate documentation, generated content should always be reviewed by a developer, solution architect, or technical lead.

The application should not expose or store sensitive information such as:

  • Passwords
  • API keys
  • Client secrets
  • Private certificates
  • Production credentials

For enterprise usage, additional controls should be added, such as:

  • Authentication and authorization
  • Audit logging
  • Secure file handling
  • Data masking
  • Approved AI model usage
  • Document versioning
  • Human approval workflow

Possible Enhancements

Future enhancements can include:

  • Direct connection to SAP Integration Suite APIs
  • Automatic extraction of adapter properties
  • Advanced iFlow XML parsing
  • Interface version comparison
  • Confluence integration
  • Jira integration
  • SAP BTP deployment
  • Approval workflow
  • Reusable client-specific TDD templates
  • Automated test case generation
  • Compliance checks against integration design standards

Conclusion

An AI-powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator can significantly improve productivity in SAP integration projects. By combining SAP CPI artifact extraction, mapping sheet analysis, AI-generated documentation, architecture diagram generation, and Word export, teams can create high-quality first drafts of technical design documents in minutes.

The key principle is to use AI as an accelerator, not as the final authority. The best approach is:

Extract facts → Generate draft → Review and edit → Approve and publish

This use case demonstrates how AI can be practically applied in SAP Integration Suite projects to reduce repetitive effort and improve documentation quality.

Source Code

GIT Repository : https://github.com/jacksnh/sap-cpi-ai-tdd-generator

entry.title: SAP Cloud Integration (CI/CPI) - Self-Healing iFlows with n8n and AI
entry.content:
A concept for AI powered integration recovery using n8n workflows
and a Large Language Model (LLM) alongside SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)

 

Introduction

This blog presents a concept for making SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) iFlows self-healing using n8n and a Large Language Model(LLM). The idea is straightforward: instead of waiting for someone to manually diagnose a failure, update a credential, and replay a message, what if the system could detect the failure, understand what went wrong, and fix the root cause automatically with human approval?

What is described here is a proof of concept built to demonstrate this idea. It focuses on one specific and very common failure scenario: an iFlow fails because a credential expired. The concept can be extended to other failure types based on your specific requirements. The goal of this blog is to share the idea, show how it can be implemented, and spark discussion about what AI-assisted integration operations could look like in practice.

Note:  This solution was built and tested on SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP. It uses n8n community edition (free, self-hosted) and OpenAI GPT-4o-mini for AI classification. It is a concept implementation, not a production-ready product.

How the Solution Works

The framework runs as four n8n workflows that sit alongside your existing Cloud Integration (CPI) tenant. No existing iFlow logic is changed. Two small additive steps are added to the iFlow error handler. Everything else runs in n8n.

What the LLM Adds

The LLM does something a regex classifier cannot: it understands context. It reads the full error text, considers the iFlow name, the credential involved, and writes a plain English explanation for the operator, suggests a root cause, and detects recurring patterns.

Example LLM output for a 401 error:

{
  "error_type":        "CREDENTIAL_EXPIRED",
  "confidence":        94,
  "risk_level":        "LOW",
  "plain_english":     "The bank API rejected the credentials because the password expired.",
  "likely_cause":      "The bank rotates credentials on a monthly cycle.",
  "recommended_action":"Update the credential and set a monthly rotation reminder.",
  "pattern_detected":  "4 credential failures in 30 days, all on the last Monday of the month."
}

Tip:  The pattern detection is the most valuable output over time. After 3 to 4 incidents, the LLM starts detecting monthly rotation cycles and recurring failure patterns that no monitoring dashboard would surface.

 

What You Need Before Starting to build your own PoC

Requirement

Details

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) tenant

Any edition of SAP Integration Suite on BTP

n8n

Free community edition, self-hosted via Docker

Docker Desktop

Free download from docker.com

OpenAI API key

From platform.openai.com (or use Anthropic/Ollama instead)

Microsoft Teams

A channel with an Incoming Webhook connector configured

CPI OAuth2 service key

Create a service key on the Process Integration Runtime instance in BTP cockpit

ngrok (free)

To expose your local n8n to the internet so CPI can call it

 

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Install n8n Using Docker

Open Terminal on your Mac and run:

docker run -d \
  --name n8n \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  -p 5678:5678 \
  -v "$(pwd)/data:/home/node/.n8n" \
  --env-file n8n_configuration_file.env \
  n8nio/n8n

Then open http://localhost:5678 in your browser and complete the setup.

Step 2: Create the Configuration File

Create a file called n8n_configuration_file.env with the following content:

N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY=your_32_char_random_key
N8N_BLOCK_ENV_ACCESS_IN_NODE=false
N8N_BASIC_AUTH_ACTIVE=false
 
CPI_BASE_URL=https://your-tenant.it-accd003.cfapps.eu12.hana.ondemand.com
TEAMS_WEBHOOK_URL=https://yourcompany.webhook.office.com/webhookb2/xxxxx
N8N_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5678
 
N8N_CRED_MOCK_BANK_BASIC_AUTH_USER=demo_user
N8N_CRED_MOCK_BANK_BASIC_AUTH_PASSWORD=demo_pass_correct

Warning:  N8N_BLOCK_ENV_ACCESS_IN_NODE=false is required. Without it you will see [ERROR: access to env vars denied] in every node that reads configuration values.

Step 3: Add the CPI OAuth2 Credential in n8n

In n8n, go to Home screen and click Credentials

1. Click Add Credential and search for OAuth2 API

2. Fill in the values from your CPI service key:

Field

Value

Credential Name

CPI OAuth2

Grant Type

Client Credentials

Access Token URL

Your tokenurl value from the service key

Client ID

Your clientid value from the service key

Client Secret

Your clientsecret value from the service key

3. Click Save

Step 4: Add the LLM Credential

  1. In n8n Credentials, click Add Credential and search for OpenAI
  2. Paste your OpenAI API key and save it as OpenAI API

Note:  You can also use Anthropic Claude or Ollama (free, on-premise). Ollama runs entirely on your own server so no data leaves your network. Swap the LLM node type after importing the workflow.

Step 5: Set Up the iFlow Registry

The registry controls which iFlows are opted in to self-healing. It is a JavaScript array embedded directly inside the Registry Gate node of the Detector workflow. An iFlow not in this list will never be auto-healed regardless of what error it produces.

After importing the Detector workflow in Step 6, open it and find the Registry Gate node. Edit the REGISTRY array to include your iFlow:

const REGISTRY = [
  {
    "iflow_name":      "YOUR_IFLOW_NAME",
    "enabled":         true,
    "heal_credential": true,
    "max_attempts":    3,
    "credential_name": "your_cpi_credential_name"
  }
];

Note:  The iflow_name must match exactly what the Groovy script sends as iflow_name (which comes from ${camelId} in the error handler). The credential_name must match exactly the name shown in CPI Security Material.

Step 6: Build Three n8n Workflows

Import the workflow JSON files in this order:

Order

File

Workflow Name

1

workflow_mock_bank.json

Cloud Integration Self Healer - Mock Bank API

2

workflow_detector.json

Cloud Integration Self Healer - Detector and Classifier

3

workflow_credential_healer.json

Cloud Integration Self Healer - Credential Healer

 

1. Cloud Integration Self Healer - Mock Bank API

This workflow is used to mock the Bank API

 

Mock Bank Webhook:

Receives the request sent from the Cloud Integration Iflow

Check Credentials:

To check if the credentials sent is correct or wrong from Cloud Integration iFlow

 

// ── MOCK BANK — Basic Auth reader ──────────────────────────
// Reads standard Authorization: Basic <base64> header.

const h       = $input.item.json.headers || {};
const authRaw = h['authorization'] || h['Authorization'] || '';

let user = '';
let pass = '';

if (authRaw.toLowerCase().startsWith('basic ')) {
  const b64     = authRaw.substring(6).trim();
  const decoded = Buffer.from(b64, 'base64').toString('utf8');
  const colon   = decoded.indexOf(':');
  if (colon > -1) {
    user = decoded.substring(0, colon);
    pass = decoded.substring(colon + 1);
  }
}

const valid = user === 'demo_user' && pass === 'demo_pass_correct';

console.log(`[MockBank] user=${user} valid=${valid}`);

if (valid) {
  return [{ json: {
    _is401: false,
    _body: '{"status":"accepted","transactionId":"TXN-1234567890","message":"Payment instruction received"}'
  }}];
} else {
  return [{ json: {
    _is401: true,
    _body: '{"error":"Unauthorized","message":"Invalid credentials — check username and password","code":"AUTH_FAILED","receivedUser":"' + user + '"}'
  }}];
}

Respond to Caller:

2. Cloud Integration Self Healer - Detector and Classifier

Whenever there is an error in Cloud Integration iFlow, this workflow can be triggered from Exception SubProcess or common Exception Handler iFlow.

Cloud Integration Failure Webhook:

Registry Gate:

The Registry Gate is a Code node that acts as the opt-in list. It checks whether the failed iFlow is registered for self-healing and attaches its configuration to the payload for all downstream nodes to use.

const REGISTRY = [
  {
    "iflow_name":      "FIN_AP_VendorInvoice",
    "enabled":         true,
    "heal_credential": true,
    "max_attempts":    3,
    "credential_name": "mock_bank_basic_auth"
  },
  {
    "iflow_name":      "LOG_Delivery_3PL",
    "enabled":         true,
    "heal_credential": false,
    "max_attempts":    1,
    "credential_name": null
  },
  {
    "iflow_name":      "HR_Replication_Daily",
    "enabled":         false,
    "heal_credential": false,
    "max_attempts":    0,
    "credential_name": null
  }
];

const payload   = $input.item.json.body || $input.item.json;
const iflowName = payload.iflow_name;

const entry = REGISTRY.find(
  function(r) { return r.iflow_name === iflowName && r.enabled === true; }
);

if (!entry) {
  return [{ json: Object.assign({}, payload, {
    _registered: false,
    _escalate_only: true,
    _registry_entry: null
  }) }];
}

return [{ json: Object.assign({}, payload, {
  _registered: true,
  _escalate_only: false,
  _registry_entry: entry
}) }];

Production note: "In this PoC the registry is hardcoded in the node. For production, consider moving it to Google Sheets or a configuration endpoint so the operations team can manage it without modifying the workflow. Similarly, the correct credential password is stored as an environment variable. For production, replace the Fetch New Credential node with a call to Azure Key Vault or your organization's secrets management solution. That is a single node change. Everything else stays the same.

Is Registered?:

Check if the iFlow is registered for self-healing.

Unregistered - Format Alert:

// Unregistered iFlow - send a minimal Teams alert to the
// default escalation channel (not the iFlow owner, since
// the registry has no owner info for this iFlow)
const item = $input.item.json;
return [{
  json: {
    alert_type: 'UNREGISTERED_IFLOW',
    iflow_name: item.iflow_name,
    message_guid: item.message_guid,
    error_text: item.error_text,
    message: `iFlow '${item.iflow_name}' failed but is not in the self-healing registry. Add it to iflow_registry.json to enable auto-healing.`
  }
}];

Dedup Guard:

It prevents the same failure from being processed twice. When a failure arrives, it checks whether the message GUID has already been seen by storing processed GUIDs in n8n workflow static data. If the same GUID arrives again within 24 hours, for example from a retry storm in CPI, it is silently dropped so the healing process does not trigger twice for the same incident.

// ── DEDUPLICATION GUARD ────────────────────────────────────
// Prevent processing the same MessageGuid twice.
// Uses n8n workflow static data as a simple in-memory store.
// For production, replace with a Postgres insert + SELECT.

const staticData = $getWorkflowStaticData('global');
if (!staticData.processedGuids) staticData.processedGuids = {};

const guid = $input.item.json.message_guid;
const now = Date.now();

if (staticData.processedGuids[guid]) {
  // Already processed - stop here
  return [];
}

// Record as processing (TTL: 24 hours to prevent unbounded growth)
staticData.processedGuids[guid] = now;

// Prune entries older than 24h
const TWENTY_FOUR_H = 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000;
for (const [key, ts] of Object.entries(staticData.processedGuids)) {
  if (now - ts > TWENTY_FOUR_H) delete staticData.processedGuids[key];
}

return [$input.item];

Prepare LLM Prompt:

It builds the prompt that will be sent to the AI model. It includes the error text, iFlow name, credential name, and the last five failures for that specific iFlow stored in workflow static data. This failure history is what allows the LLM to detect recurring patterns over time, for example noticing that the same iFlow has failed four times in 30 days always on the same day of the week.

// Builds the LLM prompt with full error context.
// Maintains a per-iFlow failure history (last 5) in static data.

const item = $input.item.json;
const reg  = item._registry_entry || {};

const staticData = $getWorkflowStaticData('global');
if (!staticData.failureHistory) staticData.failureHistory = {};
const iflow = item.iflow_name;
const hist  = staticData.failureHistory[iflow] || [];
hist.unshift({ ts: item.failure_timestamp, err: (item.error_text||'').substring(0,120) });
if (hist.length > 5) hist.pop();
staticData.failureHistory[iflow] = hist;
const historyText = hist.slice(1).map((h,i) => '  '+(i+2)+'. ['+h.ts+'] '+h.err).join('\n') || '  None';

const prompt = 'You are an SAP Integration Suite expert.\n'
  + 'Analyse this SAP Cloud Integration iFlow failure and return ONLY valid JSON with no other text.\n\n'
  + 'iFlow name: ' + item.iflow_name + '\n'
  + 'Credential name: ' + (item.credential || 'unknown') + '\n'
  + 'Error text: ' + (item.error_text || 'No error text provided') + '\n'
  + 'Recent failures on this iFlow:\n' + historyText + '\n\n'
  + 'Return this exact JSON structure:\n'
  + '{\n'
  + '  "error_type": "CREDENTIAL_EXPIRED or TRANSIENT or RATE_LIMITED or CERT_ERROR or MAPPING_ERROR or BAPI_EXCEPTION or UNKNOWN",\n'
  + '  "healing_action": "credential_heal or exponential_backoff or wait_and_retry or escalate",\n'
  + '  "confidence": <number 0-100>,\n'
  + '  "risk_level": "LOW or MEDIUM or HIGH",\n'
  + '  "safe_to_auto_heal": <true or false>,\n'
  + '  "plain_english": "<one sentence: what went wrong>",\n'
  + '  "likely_cause": "<one sentence: why it probably happened>",\n'
  + '  "recommended_action": "<one sentence: what the operator should do>",\n'
  + '  "operator_message": "<Teams card message written for a non-technical user>",\n'
  + '  "pattern_detected": "<recurring pattern across recent failures, or null>"\n'
  + '}';

if (!staticData.attempts) staticData.attempts = {};
const guid = item.message_guid;
staticData.attempts[guid] = (staticData.attempts[guid] || 0) + 1;
const maxAttempts = reg.max_attempts || 3;
const attemptCount = staticData.attempts[guid];

return [{ json: Object.assign({}, item, { _llm_prompt: prompt, attempt_count: attemptCount, max_attempts: maxAttempts }) }];

LLM Classify Error:

The LLM Classify Error node sends the prepared prompt to OpenAI GPT-4o-mini and receives a structured JSON response back. The response contains the error type, confidence score, risk level, a plain-English explanation of what went wrong, the likely cause, a recommended action, and any recurring pattern detected across previous failures.

AI Confidence Teams Card:

Inform the Operations personnel/team about the failure via Microsoft Teams Channel with iFlow details.

JSON Body:

{
  "text": "🔐 **Credential Rotation — Approval Required**\n\n**iFlow:** {{ $json.iflow_name }}\n**Message GUID:** {{ $json.message_guid }}\n\n---\n\n🤖 **AI Analysis** *({{ $json.confidence }}% confidence | Risk: {{ $json.risk_level }})*\n\n**What went wrong:** {{ $json.plain_english }}\n\n**Likely cause:** {{ $json.likely_cause }}\n\n**Recommended action:** {{ $json.recommended_action }}\n\n{{ $json.pattern_detected ? '⚠️ **Pattern detected:** ' + $json.pattern_detected + '\\n\\n' : '' }}---\n\n✅ Please review the details above and approve or reject the credential rotation in the next card."
}

Trigger Healer:

This is used to trigger "Cloud Integration Self Healer - Credential Healer" workflow.

JSON body:

{
  "message_guid":      "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json.message_guid }}",
  "iflow_name":        "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json.iflow_name }}",
  "error_text":        "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json.error_text }}",
  "credential_name":   "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json._registry_entry.credential_name }}",
  "replay_allowed":    "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json._registry_entry.replay_allowed }}",
  "max_attempts":      "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json._registry_entry.max_attempts }}",
  "heal_credential":   "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json._registry_entry.heal_credential }}",
  "confidence":        "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json.confidence }}",
  "plain_english":     "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json.plain_english }}",
  "failure_timestamp": "={{ $('Parse LLM Classification').item.json.failure_timestamp }}"
}

 

3. Cloud Integration Self Healer - Credential Healer

The Credential Healer workflow is triggered by the Detector workflow after the LLM classifies a credential failure. It sends a Teams approval card to the operator, waits for human approval, fetches the correct password from environment variables, and patches the CPI Security Material via the CPI API.

Credential Healer Webhook:

Receives the request from Detector workflow.

Identify Credential:

This node reads the credential name from the incoming payload sent by the Detector workflow. It cleans the value by stripping any leading characters added by n8n during data transfer, and generates a unique approval ID that will be used to track this specific healing session.

Java Script:

// Reads the flat credential_name field sent by the Detector workflow.
// Falls back to _registry_entry.credential_name for backwards compatibility.

const raw  = $input.item.json;
const item = raw.body && typeof raw.body === 'object' && raw.body.iflow_name
             ? raw.body
             : raw.iflow_name
             ? raw
             : raw.body || raw;

// credential_name arrives as a flat field from the Detector
const credentialName = item.credential_name
                    || item._registry_entry?.credential_name
                    || item.credential;

if (!credentialName) {
  throw new Error('No credential_name found. Ensure Trigger Healer sends credential_name as a flat field.');
}

return [{ json: Object.assign({}, item, {
  _credential_name: credentialName,
  _approval_id: 'approval_' + (item.message_guid || 'unknown') + '_' + Date.now()
}) }];

Build Approval Card:

This node constructs a Microsoft Teams MessageCard object in JavaScript with the iFlow name, credential name, AI-generated explanation, confidence score, and clickable Approve and Reject links. The approve and reject URLs use n8n's built-in $execution.resumeUrl variable which points to the current paused execution, ensuring the correct workflow instance resumes when the operator clicks either button.

Java script:

const item       = $input.item.json;
const approveUrl = $execution.resumeUrl + '&action=approve';
const rejectUrl  = $execution.resumeUrl + '&action=reject';

const card = {
  "@type": "MessageCard",
  "@context": "http://schema.org/extensions",
  "themeColor": "FF8C00",
  "summary": "Credential Rotation Approval Required",
  "sections": [{
    "activityTitle": "Credential Rotation - Approval Required",
    "facts": [
      { "name": "iFlow",        "value": item.iflow_name        || "unknown" },
      { "name": "Credential",   "value": item._credential_name  || "unknown" },
      { "name": "What went wrong", "value": item.plain_english  || item.error_text || "unknown" },
      { "name": "AI Confidence",   "value": (item.confidence    || "N/A") + "%" }
    ]
  }],
  "potentialAction": [
    {
      "@type": "OpenUri",
      "name": "Approve and Heal",
      "targets": [{ "os": "default", "uri": approveUrl }]
    },
    {
      "@type": "OpenUri",
      "name": "Reject",
      "targets": [{ "os": "default", "uri": rejectUrl }]
    }
  ]
};

return [{ json: { _card: JSON.stringify(card), _approve_url: approveUrl, _reject_url: rejectUrl } }];

Send Teams Approval Card:

This card is used to request the operation team to approve or reject the credential rotation

Wait for Human Approval:

This node pauses the workflow execution for up to 2 hours until the operator clicks either the Approve or Reject link in the Teams card, which resumes the execution and passes the chosen action as a query parameter.

Approved:

Rejected - Teams Alert:

This node is used to alert the operations team if the credential rotation is rejected.

JSON body:

{
  "text": "❌ Credential rotation  **REJECTED** - Manual Intervention Required\n\n**iFlow**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json.iflow_name }}\n**Credential**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json._credential_name }}\n\n Please update the credential manually in CPI Security Material and request source system to resend the message."
}

Fetch New Credential:

This node reads the correct username and password from n8n environment variables using the credential name as a key, following the naming convention N8N_CRED_{CREDENTIAL_NAME_UPPERCASE}_USER and N8N_CRED_{CREDENTIAL_NAME_UPPERCASE}_PASSWORD.

const item     = $('Identify Credential').item.json;
const rawCred  = (item._credential_name || '').replace(/^=/, '').trim();
const credName = rawCred.toUpperCase().replace(/-/g, '_').replace(/ /g, '_');

const user     = $env['N8N_CRED_' + credName + '_USER'];
const password = $env['N8N_CRED_' + credName + '_PASSWORD'];

if (!user || !password) {
  throw new Error(
    'Credential not found in environment. Add N8N_CRED_' + credName + '_USER and N8N_CRED_' + credName + '_PASSWORD to your .env file.'
  );
}

return [{ json: Object.assign({}, item, {
  _new_user:     user,
  _new_password: password
}) }];

Clean Credentials:

This node is used to remove any leading characters from the credential name added by n8n.

Java script:

const prevItem  = $('Fetch New Credential').item.json;
const cleanCred = (prevItem._credential_name || '').replace(/^=/, '').trim();

return [{ json: Object.assign({}, prevItem, {
  _credential_name: cleanCred
}) }];

Patch CPI Security Material:

This node is used to perform PUT operation using the standard SAP Security Content API

https://api.sap.com/api/SecurityContent/overview

JSON body

{
  "Name": "{{ $json._credential_name }}",
  "Kind": "default",
  "Description": "Updated by n8n self-healer",
  "User": "{{ $json._new_user }}",
  "Password": "{{ $json._new_password }}"
}

Verify CPI Patch:

This node is used to check the response of the CPI Patch.

Java script:

const resp = $input.item.json;
const statusCode = resp.statusCode || resp.$response?.statusCode || 200;
const success = statusCode >= 200 && statusCode < 300;

return [{ json: Object.assign({}, resp, {
  _cpi_patch_success: success,
  _cpi_patch_status:  statusCode
}) }];

Patch Ok?:

Send Teams Success Confirmation:

JSON body:

{
  "text": "✅ Credential Rotated **Successfully**\n\n**iFlow**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json.iflow_name }}\n**Credential**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json._credential_name }}\nNew password applied to CPI Security Material.\n\nNext step: Ask the source system to resend the failed message."
}

Send Teams Failure Confirmation:

JSON body:

{
  "text": "❌ Credential Patch FAILED - Manual Intervention Required\n\n**iFlow**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json.iflow_name }}\n**Credential**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json._credential_name }}\n**CPI API Status**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json._cpi_patch_status }}\n\nThe automatic credential rotation failed. Please update the credential manually in CPI Security Material and replay the message from CPI Message Monitor.\n\n**Message GUID**{{ $('Identify Credential').item.json.message_guid }}"
}

Step 7: Expose n8n Using ngrok

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) runs in the cloud. To call your local n8n it needs a public URL. Use ngrok:

brew install ngrok/ngrok/ngrok

ngrok config add-authtoken YOUR_TOKEN

ngrok http 5678

Copy the https URL shown by ngrok (example: https://abc123.ngrok-free.app).

Warning:  ngrok free tier gives a different URL every time it restarts. Update the CPI iFlow HTTP adapter URL whenever ngrok restarts.

Testing:

To test the set up, I have created a simple iFlow in Cloud Integration.

If have used a security material named "mock_bank_basic_auth". I have configured our n8n mock bank api workflow as the receiver. Since it is self-hosted, BTP can't reach the localhost. Hence, I have used ngrok and it gave me the host as "https://parchment-purely-zoologist.ngrok-free.dev/webhook/mock-bank-api"

In the Exception subprocess, I am calling n8n "Cloud Integration Self Healer - Detector and Classifier" workflow and as an input I am using the below groovy script to build the error payload.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import org.apache.camel.Exchange
import org.apache.camel.builder.SimpleBuilder

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def headers  = message.getHeaders()
    def props    = message.getProperties()
    Exchange exchange = message.exchange

    // Get iFlow runtime ID via camelId
    def iflowId = SimpleBuilder.simple('${camelId}')
            .evaluate(exchange, String)
            ?.toString()
            ?.trim() ?: 'unknown'

    def errorMsg = 'Unknown error'
    try {
        errorMsg = props.get('CamelExceptionCaught')?.getMessage() ?: 'Unknown error'
    } catch (Exception e) {}

    def payload = [
        message_guid      : headers.get('SAP_MessageProcessingLogID') ?: 'unknown',
        iflow_name        : iflowId,
        error_text        : errorMsg,
        credential        : props.get('credentialName')               ?: '',
        correlation_id    : headers.get('SAP_MessageProcessingLogID') ?: '',
        failure_timestamp : new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss")
    ]

    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(payload))
    message.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json')
    return message
}

With the correct credential, I receive the response as below.

If I edit the security material with the wrong password then because HTTP 401 error, my exception subprocess will call n8 workflow and I receive a Approval card in MS Teams as below.

If I Reject then I receive an alert as below 

If I Approve then I receive an alert as below and the correct/new credentials are updated in Cloud Integration Security material for the credential "mock_bank_basic_auth".

Moving to Production

Replace Demo Credentials with a Secrets Vault

For the PoC, credentials are stored as environment variables in the n8n configuration file. For production, replace the Fetch New Credential code node with an HTTP call to Azure Key Vault or HashiCorp Vault. This is a single node change. The rest of the workflow stays identical.

 

Security Considerations

Risk

Mitigation

Credential patching without audit trail

n8n logs every execution with timestamp and approval action

n8n webhook exposed publicly via ngrok

Add IP allowlisting in ngrok or use a reverse proxy with authentication

Summary

This blog presented a concept for self-healing SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) iFlows using n8n and AI. The idea is to detect credential failures instantly, use an LLM to understand the root cause in context, notify the operator with a plain-English explanation, and automatically fix the credential configuration after human approval.

This is a concept and a starting point. Every organization has different requirements, different tooling preferences, and different security policies. The patterns shown here can be adapted, extended, and built upon. Some teams may want to add a secrets vault. Others may want to extend the error classifier to handle additional failure types. Others may want to replace n8n with SAP Build Process Automation as the orchestration layer. The core idea remains the same regardless of which tools you choose.

I hope this sparks some ideas. If you build on this concept or extend it in an interesting direction, I would love to hear about it in the comments.

entry.title: CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite / Managing parameters and log levels
entry.content:
The previous post walked through the Download → Git → Deploy pipeline from button press to a fully deployed package on TST. Near the end it mentioned that two JSON files are created alongside the package source in Git: one holding externalized parameter values, one controlling deployment behaviour. This post covers both in depth — what they contain, how they stay fresh across downloads, and how to use the dedicated workflow that lets you update a single iFlow’s parameters without triggering a full download and redeploy cycle.

  • Why Configuration Lives in Git
  • ConfigParams_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json — Parameters as Code
    • File Structure
    • How the Merge Works
  • Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json — Deployment Control
    • File Structure
    • The Priority Chain
  • Updating Parameters Without a Full Redeploy
    • What You Fill In
    • What Happens Inside
  • Log Level Management
  • Runtime Targeting — What’s Coming
  • Key Takeaways

Why Configuration Lives in Git

Externalized parameters are one of the most valuable features of SAP Integration Suite. They let developers factor out everything that changes between environments — endpoint URLs, user names, behaviour flags, retry counts — and manage them separately from the iFlow logic itself. But they create a challenge for any deployment pipeline: the values stored on the DEV tenant are not the values you want on TST or PRD.

The solution is straightforward: store all parameter values in Git, one set per environment, alongside the package source. When the pipeline deploys a package, it reads the correct values from Git and applies them to the target tenant via the SAP Integration Suite API. No manual step. No configuration drift. No post-deployment checklist.

The same principle applies to deployment control. Whether a specific iFlow should be activated on a given environment — and at what log level — is a team decision that belongs in Git, not in someone’s head. Both decisions are recorded and version-controlled.

All configuration files for a package live in a dedicated subfolder alongside the package source:

IntegrationPackages/
  PackageName~PackageID/
    Configuration/
      ConfigParams_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json
      Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json
    [artifact source files]

ConfigParams_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json — Parameters as Code

File Structure

There is one ConfigParams_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json per package. It covers every iFlow in the package that has externalized parameters. The structure groups parameters by artifact, and for each parameter holds a ParameterValues object containing the values for every environment the team manages.

{
  "PackageIntegrationFlowParameters": {
    "PackageName": "Finance Integration",
    "PackageID": "CORP_FINANCE",
    "Artifacts": [
      {
        "ArtifactID": "CORP_FINANCE_INVOICE_POSTING",
        "ArtifactName": "Invoice Posting",
        "ArtifactType": "INTEGRATION_FLOW",
        "Parameters": [
          {
            "ParameterKey": "API_ENDPOINT",
            "DataType": "xsd:string",
            "Description": "Target system endpoint URL",
            "ParameterValues": {
              "Default": "https://api.dev.example.com/invoices",
              "TST": "https://api.tst.example.com/invoices",
              "PRD": "https://api.prd.example.com/invoices"
            }
          },
          {
            "ParameterKey": "RETRY_COUNT",
            "DataType": "xsd:integer",
            "Description": "Number of retry attempts",
            "ParameterValues": {
              "Default": "3"
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

The Default value mirrors what the developer has configured on the DEV tenant at the time of the last download. It updates automatically on every download. The stage-specific values — TST, PRD, or whatever environments the team has configured — are managed by the team directly in Git. They are committed alongside the package source and never overwritten by a download.

When the pipeline applies parameters to a target environment, value resolution is simple: if a key matching the environment name exists in ParameterValues, it is used. If not, the pipeline falls back to Default. A parameter like RETRY_COUNT that is the same everywhere needs only the Default entry. A parameter like API_ENDPOINT that differs per environment gets one entry per stage.

Adding a new environment — to add a PRD stage, add the environment key to the relevant parameters in the JSON file and commit. The pipeline reads whatever keys are present; no workflow changes are needed. Adding an entry for TST to a parameter that previously had only a Default value is a one-line change, visible in the commit diff, and effective on the next pipeline run.

How the Merge Works

The file is created on the first download of a package, populated with the parameter values currently live on the DEV tenant. On every subsequent download, the pipeline performs a smart merge: it takes the fresh parameter values from BTP as the new Default entries — reflecting whatever the developer has set on DEV since the last download — while leaving every stage-specific value already present in Git untouched.

This means two things happen simultaneously on a download. The developer’s changes to parameter values on DEV are captured automatically. And the team’s environment-specific configuration for TST, PRD, and any other stage is never at risk of being overwritten. Both are preserved without manual intervention.

If a parameter is added to an iFlow on DEV, it appears in Git on the next download with only a Default value. The team then adds the stage-specific values at their own pace, committing them to the development branch before promoting to that environment.

Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json — Deployment Control

File Structure

There is one Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json per package. It contains an entry for every iFlow in the package, controlling whether the iFlow is activated on a given environment and at what log level.

Only iFlows are controlled here. Message Mappings, Script Collections, and Value Mappings are always deployed automatically as part of the package — they have no entry in this file and no per-environment toggle. The selective deployment behaviour described in this section applies exclusively to Integration Flows.

{
  "PackageIntegrationFlowDeployments": {
    "PackageName": "Finance Integration",
    "PackageID": "CORP_FINANCE",
    "IntegrationFlows": [
      {
        "ArtifactID": "CORP_FINANCE_INVOICE_POSTING",
        "ArtifactName": "Invoice Posting",
        "Deploy": "false",
        "Rank": "100",
        "LogLevel": "INFO",
        "Runtimes": "iflmap"
      },
      {
        "ArtifactID": "CORP_FINANCE_CREDIT_CHECK",
        "ArtifactName": "Credit Check",
        "Deploy": "false",
        "Rank": "100",
        "LogLevel": "INFO",
        "Runtimes": "iflmap",
        "Environments": {
          "TST": { "Deploy": "true", "LogLevel": "INFO" },
          "PRD": { "Deploy": "true", "LogLevel": "ERROR" }
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

The file is created on the first download with safe defaults: Deploy is false, LogLevel is INFO. On subsequent downloads, the file is left unchanged — the team’s deployment decisions persist across downloads just like the parameter values. The pipeline updates the ArtifactName field if the display name changes on BTP, but all other settings are preserved.

The four top-level fields on each entry have the following meaning:

Deploy — whether this iFlow is activated during a deployment run. Setting it to false means the iFlow is uploaded to the design time as part of the package but never activated at runtime. This is useful during development, or for iFlows that are intentionally inactive in a given environment.

Rank — the deployment sequence. When a package has iFlows with dependencies — one flow must be running before another is activated — lower Rank values deploy first. Flows with the same Rank are deployed in alphabetical order of their ArtifactName. Entries without a Rank field are deployed first, also in alphabetical order, before any ranked entries. New entries created by the pipeline get a default of 100, which is appropriate for most scenarios.

LogLevel — the operational log level for this iFlow after deployment. This is the level the pipeline sets on the runtime after activation. The detail is covered below.

Runtimes — the target runtime identifier. Currently defaults to iflmap, which represents Cloud Integration. This value is a placeholder — the final identifiers for both Cloud Integration and Edge Integration Cell will most likely be revised once SAP publishes the public APIs that drive runtime targeting. The field is already part of the file format so that the configuration is in place when the functionality goes live; the detail is at the end of this post.

The Priority Chain

A simple global default is often not enough. An iFlow might be disabled globally but explicitly enabled for TST and PRD. A log level might be set to INFO on TST for observability but ERROR on PRD for performance reasons. The Environments block on each iFlow entry handles exactly this.

When the pipeline resolves whether to deploy an iFlow to a given environment, it applies a three-level priority chain:

  1. Environments.<env>.Deploy — most specific, takes precedence if present
  2. A flat top-level key named after the environment (legacy format, still supported)
  3. Deploy — the global default, used when no environment-specific override exists

The same chain applies to LogLevel and Runtimes, falling back to the top-level values when no environment-specific entry is found.

In the example above, CORP_FINANCE_INVOICE_POSTING has Deploy: false globally and no Environments block. It will not be activated on any environment — uploaded to the design time as part of the package, but never started at runtime. CORP_FINANCE_CREDIT_CHECK also has Deploy: false globally, but the Environments block adds explicit overrides for TST and PRD: deployed on both, with log level INFO on TST for observability and ERROR on PRD for performance. DEV inherits the global false, so the iFlow is not activated on DEV either.

Keeping an iFlow inactive by default but active on specific environments — set Deploy: false globally and add "TST": { "Deploy": "true" } to the Environments block. The iFlow is then inactive on any environment without an explicit override — including DEV in case of a re-import — and only activated where the block explicitly says so.

Updating Parameters Without a Full Redeploy

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow is the right tool when you want to move an updated package from DEV to TST. But sometimes the need is more targeted: a team member has updated a parameter value on the development branch in Git — changed an endpoint URL, corrected a configuration flag — and needs that change applied to a running iFlow on a specific environment without going through a full download cycle. That is what the Update Externalized Parameters for IFlow workflow is for.

Only the development branch is supported. The workflow always reads from development — there is no input to select a different branch. Any parameter change a team wants to push must be committed to development first. Branch selection may be introduced in a later release as more advanced landscape scenarios call for it; for now, the same simple branch model as the Download → Git → Deploy workflow applies.

What You Fill In

The workflow is triggered manually with three inputs:

  • Environment — the target environment where the update should be applied. The dropdown is populated automatically from the configured GitHub Environments.
  • IFlow ID — the artifact ID of the specific iFlow to update. The pipeline uses this to locate the package automatically — the team does not need to know or enter the package ID.
  • Evaluate Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json — a choice between false and true. Setting this to true tells the pipeline to check the deployment configuration and, if the iFlow is marked for deployment in the target environment, redeploy it after applying the parameter changes. Setting it to false updates the parameters without touching the runtime.

  The three inputs are all the team needs. The pipeline resolves the package, locates the configuration file, and applies the values — no package ID or file path required.

What Happens Inside

The pipeline runs in two jobs. The first is always executed; the second is conditional.

Job 1: Update Parameters. The pipeline checks out the development branch, resolves the package ID from the iFlow ID by querying the BTP API, and reads the ConfigParams_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json for that package.
