It then iterates over every parameter defined for the iFlow and applies the correct value to the BTP design time via the SAP Integration Suite API — using the stage-specific value if one exists, falling back to Default otherwise. Each parameter update is a separate API call. The pipeline logs every call: the parameter key, the value applied, and the HTTP response.

The update targets the design time. Parameters on the design time are the source of truth for the runtime: they are applied when the iFlow is next deployed. To push them to a currently running iFlow immediately, the second job is needed.

Job 2: Deploy (conditional). If Evaluate Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json was set to true, the pipeline reads the Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json for the package and resolves the deploy decision for this iFlow in the target environment using the priority chain described above. If the iFlow is marked for deployment, it is activated. If not, the job completes without deploying. After activation, the log level is set to the configured value for the environment.

This design gives the team control over the tradeoff. A parameter change that takes effect on the next scheduled deployment does not need an immediate redeploy. A critical correction that needs to be live right now gets both the parameter update and the redeploy in a single workflow run.

Log Level Management

Every iFlow deployed by the pipeline starts from a known state: when an artifact is newly deployed to SAP Integration Suite, the platform sets its log level to INFO by default. That default is fine for steady-state operation, but teams often want a different level — ERROR on busy environments to reduce noise, WARN as a standard operational baseline, or DEBUG temporarily when troubleshooting a specific flow.

Without automation, the log level requires a manual visit to the SAP Integration Suite UI after every deployment. With this pipeline, the log level is set automatically as part of every deployment, driven by the LogLevel field in the Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json. No cockpit visit required. No post-deployment checklist. The pipeline handles it.

The log level API used here is an internal BTP endpoint. It is not part of the official public API surface, which means it could change without notice. The pipeline therefore provides a safety switch: setting the repository variable BTP_SET_LOG_LEVEL to false disables log level management entirely. If the endpoint ever breaks after a BTP update, one variable change stops the failures without modifying the workflow. The feature defaults to enabled. The pipeline supports three log level values for the post-deployment call: ERROR, WARN, and NONE. INFO is omitted on purpose because it is already the BTP default after a fresh deployment — making the call would be a no-op. DEBUG and TRACE are not part of the supported set and are not applied if entered in the JSON; they remain available for temporary manual adjustment via the SAP Integration Suite UI.

Log level INFO is skipped. Since BTP sets newly deployed iFlows to INFO by default, the pipeline skips the API call when the configured log level is INFO. The result is already correct and no call is needed. The call is only made for ERROR, WARN, or NONE, after the deployment status reaches STARTED.

Log level management currently applies to the Cloud Integration runtime. The log level identifier used internally will be aligned with the runtime-specific values once SAP’s public APIs for Edge Integration Cell provide the necessary endpoint.

Runtime Targeting — What’s Coming

The Runtimes field in the deployment configuration is already part of the file format. It is intended to identify which runtime an iFlow runs on — Cloud Integration or Edge Integration Cell. The field is populated on every new deployment file with a default value and is preserved on every subsequent download.

The current default is iflmap. This is a working placeholder, not a final value — both the Cloud Integration identifier and the Edge Integration Cell identifier will most likely be replaced with the values SAP defines once the public APIs for runtime targeting are released. In the current release, the field is read and logged during deployment but does not yet influence where the iFlow is deployed or how the log level is set. The infrastructure is in place; the functional behaviour is waiting for SAP to publish the public APIs for Edge Integration Cell runtime management.

Once those APIs are available, the Runtimes field will drive both deployment target selection and log level management across runtimes. Existing deployment configuration files will need their runtime identifier replaced with the final SAP value — a one-time global search-and-replace, not a manual per-iFlow change — but the field itself, the priority chain, and the per-environment override mechanism are all already in place.

Key Takeaways

  • Both configuration files live in Configuration/ inside the package directory in Git. There is one ConfigParams_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json and one Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json per package, covering all iFlows in the package.
  • The parameter file is updated on every download: Default values reflect DEV, stage-specific values are preserved. The deployment file is never overwritten on download; all team decisions persist.
  • The Update Externalized Parameters workflow targets a single iFlow by ID. The pipeline resolves the package automatically. The optional second job redeploys the iFlow if its deployment configuration says it should be active in the target environment.
  • Log levels are set automatically after every deployment, driven by the Deployment_INTEGRATION_FLOW.json configuration. The BTP_SET_LOG_LEVEL variable provides a safety switch if the internal API endpoint changes.
  • The Runtimes field in the deployment configuration is already present in the file format. The current value iflmap is a working placeholder — both the Cloud Integration and Edge Integration Cell identifiers will most likely be revised once SAP publishes the public APIs for runtime targeting.

The next post in this series covers the Release Import workflow: how Git tags and branch comparisons are used to calculate exactly what has changed since the last release, and how only that delta is promoted to production.

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

Series overview: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations

entry.title: Creating a cloud MCP using SAP API Management and SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

Nowadays most of us use agents serving cloud or local models. All these agents must be provided with a list of tools to fully achieve their potential. Going for tools inevitably takes us to the path of searching for open or closed-source MCPs, projects that, in some cases, need to be deployed locally. This translates into a need of reliability, control, full-time availability and extra hassle.

When it comes to local usage, all of these do not transform into critical factors when deciding the architecture, if we want to use an MCP, we just start a container, use the MCP and stop it back if we don’t need it anymore.

When we talk about real productive systems, especially SAP-class ERP systems where the percentage allocated for these critical factors is much higher, there is no room for “let’s wait it to start and try again” or “are we sure this is secure enough?”.

With this in mind, I embarked on a journey to create an MCP that can leverage an existing SAP BTP infrastructure – secure, available and scalable.

How an MCP is initializing in background

Let’s say a cloud MCP is configured in IDE. As soon as the MCP is started, IDE will send json-rpc format messages towards the MCP endpoint, looking for a new session, a tools list, it will try to keep the session alive, and it might also want to use a tool. All these actions means that the cloud MCP will have to return a proper body that will have to adhere to the MCP format for IDE to use it.

Types of messages

Initialize

This is the initial message that IDE will send.

{"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2025-11-25","capabilities":{"elicitation":{"form":{}},"roots":{"listChanged":false},"extensions":{"io.modelcontextprotocol/ui":{"mimeTypes":["text/html;profile=mcp-app"]}}},"clientInfo":{"name":"cursor-vscode","version":"1.0.0"}},"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":0}

The response that it should receive is of this format.

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": ${property.mcpJsonRpcId}, "result": { "protocolVersion": "2025-11-25", "capabilities": { "tools": {} }, "serverInfo": { "name": "mcp-name", "version": "1.0.0" } }

Tools list

After receiving the initialize response, it will try to scan for tools.

{"method":"tools/list","jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1}

The IDE will need a response like the below.

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": "${header.rpc_id}", "result": { "tools": [ { "name": "tool-1-example", "description": "Brief description of tool-1-example.", "inputSchema": { "type": "object", "properties": { "field-1": { "type": "string", "description": "a field in our tool" } }, "required": [ "field-1" ] } }, { "name": "tool-2-example", "description": "Brief description of tool-2-example.", "inputSchema": { "type": "object", "properties": { "field-1": { "type": "string", "description": "a first field in our tool" }, "field-2": { "type": "string", "description": "a second field in our tool" } }, "required": [ "field-1",”field-2” ] } } ] }

This provides all the details of our tools and how to use them.

Tools call

This endpoint provides the response of the actual tool called, so it depends a lot on the actual tool format. In broader terms, the response should still adhere to the json-rpc format, similar to this

[ jsonrpc: "2.0", id : parseMcpId(mcpIdRaw), result : [ content: [[ type: "text", text: JsonOutput.toJson(payload) ]], isError: !sentOk ] ]

Ping / keep-alive

This message offers the persistence for our MCP session.

{"method":"notifications/initialized","jsonrpc":"2.0"}

After initialization and tool calling, the MCP would stick to the same session, and it will chain messages as the agent does more tool calls.

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": ${property.mcpJsonRpcId}, "result": { "protocolVersion": "2025-11-25", "capabilities": { "tools": {} }, "serverInfo": { "name": "mcp-name", "version": "1.0.0" } }

 

Technical overview

Architecture diagram

Architecture components

SAP API Management

This is the component that exposes the MCP Gateway to the outside world and controls what agent has access to which resource/endpoint.

By default, the main endpoint served by SAP API Management is ‘https://<host>.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/mcp/v1’. Depending on what MCP the user wants to initialize, he will append it in the url like ‘/mcp/v1/websearch or ‘/mcp/v1/servicenow’.

The policies in the Pre/PostFlow of the ProxyEndpoint mostly are for checking the authorizations, roles or the needed access to the mcp that is initialized.

 

On the other hand, the policies in the TargetEndpoint are mainly for routing to the correct MCP. Based on the resulting authorizations, it will extract the initializing parameters sent in json-rpc format, and route the message to the specific SAP Integration Suite artifact.

// Pass query parameters to backend var queryParams = context.getVariable("request.querystring"); // Fetch the extracted method from the ExtractVariables policy const mcpMethod = context.getVariable('mcpMethod'); // Fetch the proxy pathsuffix (e.g., "/servicenow") const pathSuffix = context.getVariable("proxy.pathsuffix") || ""; // Set the path suffix if (mcpMethod === "initialize") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/init" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/list") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/list" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/call") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/call" + pathSuffix; } else { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/default" + pathSuffix; } // Returns the runtime hostname // const hostname = context.getVariable(‘Hostname'); var completeUrl = hostname.concat(targetUrlPath); if (queryParams) { completeUrl = completeUrl + "?" + queryParams; } // Set target url to which the message will be sent to context.setVariable('target.url', completeUrl);

As you can see, depending on the MCP method, we have several endpoints which will be communicating with the agent and returning what a real MCP would return.

This is the important part that offers us a modular approach

// Set the path suffix if (mcpMethod === "initialize") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/init" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/list") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/list" + pathSuffix; } else if (mcpMethod === "tools/call") { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/call" + pathSuffix; } else { targetUrlPath = "/http/mcp-gateway-tools/default" + pathSuffix; }

By extracting the mcpMethod out of the json-rpc calls, we will be able to use the proper path in the URL, which in the end will be transformed into a ProcessDirect link.

SAP Integration Suite MCP Gateway

 

This is the HTTPS endpoint that SAP API Management guards and communicates with. Here, based on the target endpoint set by the API policies, we are creating a property that becomes a ProcessDirect link, and our Request-Reply step just forwards it to the correct process.

Here you can see how the Url and the ContextPath are coming over from SAP API Management, and in the end, they’re the parameters used for routing to a specific ProcessDirect consumer.

SAP Integration Suite MCP Interaction

In here, as you’ve figured out by now, we have several main processes that serve as endpoints for each of our MCP actions.

Most of the needed return bodies are set by Content Modifiers that reuse the rpcId from the initial message. For the tool calling part, we have some extra things like extracting the parameters and generating the actual API format, and also, the script that converts the real API response into a proper json-rpc response expected by our agent/IDE.

Initializing the MCP into the IDE

Most of the IDEs if not all follow the mcp.json file principle with/without a fancy UI built on top of it. To initialize an MCP, you only need to fill it with something similar to this :

{ "mcpServers": { "dummy-mcp-1": { "url": "https://<host>.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/mcp/v1/dummymcp1", "headers": { "Authorization": "xxx", } }, "dummy-mcp-2": { "url": "https://<host>.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/mcp/v1/dummymcp2", "headers": { "Authorization": "xxx", "Parameter1": "Lorem", "Parameter2 ": "Ipsum” } } } }

What you see here are 2 MCPs, one initialized with just the authorization, and a second one with headers, meaning parameters that we will enforce later in our process.

Enforcing parameters and controlling tool calls

By going with this approach, we could prevent the agent from inferring things we don’t want.

If for example we don’t trust enough our agent to infer a group-id, an email address or a very specific parameter, we could enforce these parameters by adding them as headers.

SAP API Management will route these headers into our iFlows, where, our carefully-crafted groovyscripts will wait for these parameters, and in case they’re coming, the tool calls will be overwritten and the ‘hardcoded’ values will be always sent.

Excluding enforced parameters from tool definitions

If we set some parameters ourselves directly from MCP initialization, it means that our agent should not care about them. Agent knows already all the parameters from the tools-list step, so how would we prevent it from filling these as well ? Simple, 2 different tools lists : one with all the parameters displayed, and one with only what we want the agent to fill. If we want an email MCP to marian@company.com and we want the agent just to send an email with the text ‘Hello from IDE’, we enforce the recipient as a header, and the agent will see that it only needs to call the tool send-mail with the ‘email-body’ parameter.

Features

Flexibility

Designed for easy deployment of new MCPs, the solution offers a template for the iFlow that implies modifying the ProcessDirect links. The groovyscript that generates the API calls can be either set to just extract the parameters from the json-rpc payload or directly generate an URL that will be used in the RequestReply step.

Also, for the API Management part, we only need to whitelist the new resources in the kvm tables and generate a new service key in case it needs extra security.

Security

By leveraging SAP API Management as the middle men between the IDE and the MCP, we can make sure that we enforce things like load limits, spike alerts, authentication and access to only specific endpoints.

Also, the authentication to the end APIs itself is maintained in SAP Integration Suite, far away from the agent.

Control over parameters

By passing headers over to SAP Integration Suite from the MCP init in the IDE, we can enforce specific parameters and we can take out the stress from the model, which was supposed to infer these values. We all know that sometimes it’s not that good, especially when running low on context, and by having our important values stored and hardcoded outside the agent assures us that everything will be the same, everytime.

Conclusion

By using this approach, you will offer your agents an MCP that is flexible, can be secure and fully available at all time. Besides all these, maybe one of the most important perks of it it’s the fact that it can be controlled with ease, enforcing specific parameters can simplify workflows and instructions. SAP Integration Suite already offers built-in tools to transform the incoming payloads into proper formats expected by the real APIs. Groovyscript delivers as expected, offering a set of libraries that can help us manipulate the message and extract key parameters for it. On top of it, SAP API Management acts like a bouncer that checks for IDs, roles, grants access to specific resources and controls the traffic.

If you have any questions, feel free to reply here, or even go to the dedicated Q&A Section for SAP Integration Suite.

Thank you for your time !

entry.title: How to Set Up B2B Outbound Integration with Trading Partner Management in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
1. Introduction

If you work in integration long enough, you hear the same prediction again and again, EDI is going away. In reality, that has not happened. APIs and events have absolutely changed the landscape, but in many B2B scenarios, EDI is still very much alive. For a lot of companies, it remains one of the most important ways to exchange business documents with partners.

SAP’s B2B integration story has also changed quite a bit over the years. Many teams started with Seeburger on SAP XI/PI, then moved to the SAP B2B add-on on PO. In the cloud world, SAP Cloud Integration has gone through a few stages as well from basic converters, to Integration Advisor with MIGs and MAGs, and now to Trading Partner Management (TPM) together with B2B monitoring.

Because of that evolution, TPM can feel harder than it really is, especially when you first open it. It involved MIGs, agreements, identifiers, communication channels, templates, standard iFlows, custom mapping flows, and runtime processing. With so many changes over the years, it is understandable that EDI on Cloud Integration can seem complex at first.

That is exactly why I wanted to write this. Instead of starting with a complex multi-partner setup, I think it is much easier to begin with a simple outbound scenario and understand the core building blocks first. Once that mental model is clear, the rest of the setup becomes much more manageable.

In this post, I will focus on a basic outbound flow and walk through the main setup steps in SAP Integration Suite.

What “Outbound” means in TPM

In an outbound scenario, company initiates the message exchange:

  1. A business document is created in the backend, often as an IDoc from ERP or SAP S/4HANA.
  2. IDoc is sent to Cloud Integration.
  3. Cloud Integration transforms it into an EDI message, for example an X12 850
  4. Envelopes it into an interchange and delivers it to the trading partner.
  5. Optionally you can also support functional acknowledgements (for example, X12 997).

2.    End to End Setup Overview in Cloud Integration

When you look at TPM for the first time, one of the biggest questions is usually What needs to be done, and in what order?

For a basic outbound EDI setup, this is the sequence I typically follow:

  1. Create MIGs for IDoc, X12, or EDIFACT
  2. Create the required number ranges
  3. Deploy and configure the standard TPM iFlows
  4. Build the custom mapping iFlow
  5. Define your own company profile
    • identifiers
    • systems
    • communications
  6. Define the trading partner profile
    • identifiers
    • systems
    • communications
  7. Create agreement templates
  8. Create agreements and B2B scenarios
  9. Perform end-to-end testing

There are a lot of individual objects in that list, but once you work through them in sequence, the setup starts to make sense.

3.    Integration Advisor

Before you get into agreements, routing, or acknowledgements, you need the message structures in place. In SAP Integration Suite, that starts with Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs).

3.1 Create MIGs for IDoc / X12 / EDIFACT

MIGs matter because they act as the structural definition for the messages TPM works with. They are used in validation, routing, and transaction configuration.

3.1.1  Steps to Create Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs) for IDoc

To create an IDoc MIG:

  1. Navigate to Design → MIGs.

     

  2. Create a new MIG

     

  3. Choose the standard type system for SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc. Search for the message type you need, for example ORDERS.ORDERS05. Select the appropriate version, such as S/4HANA 1709. You can skip uploading a payload if you do not need it for this step.

     

     

     

     

  4. When naming the MIG, make sure the direction fits your use case:

    • IN = the MIG is used as the source
    • OUT = the MIG is used as the target
    • BOTH = the same MIG may be used in either direction

       

  5. Under Own Business Context, select the relevant Country/Region. Then click on Create.
    Examples:
    X12: United States
    EDIFACT: United Kingdom
    IDoc: United States and United Kingdom (as applicable)

     

     

  6. Right-click the node, select required fields, then Save and Activate.

     

     

3.1.2 Steps to Create Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs) for X12

In this example, the outbound IDoc is mapped to X12 850 version 5010. That means you also need a second MIG that represents the X12 target structure.

The process is basically the same as the IDoc MIG creation. The main point is that both the source and target structures are defined clearly before you continue.

 

 

4 Cloud Integration

4.1   Deploy Standard Trading Partner Management Iflows

  1. Navigate to Discover → Integrations.

     

  2. Search for “Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2”. Select the package, then click the Copy button in the top-right corner.

     

     

  3. Go to Design → Integration & APIs. Search for “Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2”, select the package, and deploy the required integration flows.
  4. Depending on the connectivity setup, deploy the following standard content as-is (no changes):
    Reusable Groovy Scripts V2
    Step 1 - Sender AS2 Communication Flow V2
    Step 1 - Sender IDOC Communication Flow V2
    Step 1 - Sender Process Direct Communication Flow V2
    Step 1b - Write Message to Message Queue
    Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow V2
    Step 3 - Receiver Communication Flow V2
    Note: Based on your connectivity model, only the relevant Step 1 sender flow needs to be deployed. However, the following are mandatory in all cases:
    • Reusable Groovy Scripts
    • Step 1b
    • Step 2
    • Step 3

    These iFlows provide the standard runtime pipeline used by TPM for routing, enveloping, and message delivery.

4.2 Create Custom Mapping Flow

In Cloud Integration, there are two main transformation approaches:

  • Message Mapping
  • Mapping Guidelines (MAG)

In this scenario, we use Message Mapping to convert data from IDoc to EDI and vice versa.

So the custom iFlow is responsible for transforming the IDoc into the target EDI structure. TPM then calls that iFlow at runtime through a Process Direct endpoint. When you configure the agreement later, you reference this custom mapping using a mapping exit.

A simple way to think about it is:

  • Standard TPM content = routing, interchange handling, delivery
  • Custom mapping iFlow = your document transformation logic

That separation helped me a lot when I was trying to understand where each piece belongs.

 

 

 

 

 

 

4.3  Create Number Ranges

Next, create the number ranges required for interchange or transaction control.

 

 

Create 5a number range and provide the following details:

  1. Name: Number range name as referenced in Trading Partner Management
  2. Description
  3. Minimum Value
  4. Maximum Value
  5. Field Length: If set to 0, the system derives the length from the maximum value
  6. Rotate: Indicates whether numbering should restart from the minimum value after the maximum is reached

If you do not want a separate number range for every partner, you can also create default number ranges and reuse them across multiple agreements.

 

 

5. Trading Partner Management

5.1  Define “Self” Company Profile in Trading Partner Management

5.1.1 Create Company Profile

Create a new company profile for your own organization, for example SELF_COMPANY.

Note: Only one company profile can exist per tenant.

 

 

 

5.1.2  Create Identifiers (for Self)

You can maintain multiple identifiers for your self-company profile. In this example, we create two:

  • ERP Identifier– DR1CLNT100 (SNDPRN)
    • Identification: DR1CLNT100
    • Alias: DR1CLNT100
    • Type System: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
    • Scheme: N/A

       

  • EDI Identifier – SELF_1234
    • Identification: SELF_1234
    • Alias: SELF_1234
    • Type System: ASC X12
    • Scheme: ZZ

       

5.1.3 Create Systems (for Self)

Under the Systems tab, create the backend system definition.

The naming is flexible, but I would recommend keeping it consistent and easy to recognize, especially once you have multiple landscapes. 

In this example:

  • Name: S4
  • Alias: S4
  • Type: Create New in this case
    • Name: S4
    • Description: S4
    • Deployment Type: OnPremise
    • Application: S4
  • Purpose: Dev

     

     

     

5.1.3.1  Create Type Systems ( for Self)

Create the relevant type system, for example:

  • Name: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
  • Version: your SAP S/4HANA release

If you are not sure which version to use, the important thing is that it aligns with the DOCREL value in the IDoc control record EDI_DC40.

 

 

 

5.1.3.1  Create Communications

Since SAP S/4HANA is sending the IDoc into Cloud Integration, create a sender communication channel such as:

  • Name: SND_IDOC
  • Alias: SND_IDOC
  • Direction: Sender
  • Adapter: IDOC

     

5.2  Define Trading Partner in Trading Partner Management

After your self-company is ready, create the external partner profile.

5.2.1  Create Trading Partners

Go to Design → B2B Scenarios. Under the Partner Profile tab, choose Create Profile.

Example:

  • Partner Name: PARTNER1
  • Company Short Name: PARTNER1

     

     

5.2.2 Create Identifiers (for Trading Partner)

You can maintain multiple identifiers for each trading partner. In this example, we create two:

  • ERP Identifier– PARTNER1(RCVPRN)
    • Identification: PARTNER1
    • Alias: PARTNER1
    • Type System: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
    • Scheme: N/A

       

  • EDI Identifier – PART_123
    • Identification: PART_123
    • Alias: PART_123
    • Type System: ASC X12
    • Scheme: ZZ

       

5.2.3  Create System (for Trading Partner)

Create a system entry for the trading partner. This represents the external side of the exchange and is later connected to the communication details.

Create a System for Trading Partner for PARTNER1.

 

 

 

 

5.2.3.1  Create Type System (for Trading Partner)

Create a Type System for the Trading Partner with the Below details

  • Name: ASC X12
  • Type: 005010

     

5.2.3.2   Create Communications (for Trading Partner)

Since the trading partner receives the outbound EDI from Cloud Integration, create a receiver communication channel.

  • Name: RCV_PD
  • Alias: RCV_PD
  • Direction: Receiver
  • Adapter: ProcessDirect
  • Address: /PD

     

5.3 Agreement Template

Agreement templates are optional, but they become very useful once you start scaling.

If you only have one partner and one transaction, they may feel like extra setup. But if you expect multiple partners or multiple message types, templates help you standardize the overall agreement structure and reduce repeated configuration.

 

 

 

 

5.4 Agreements

Agreements are where the relationship between your company, the partner, and the business transaction is actually defined.

To create one:

  • Go to the Agreement tab and click on create.

     

  • Select the agreement template, for example SAP_PARTNER

     

  • Choose Copy from Template

     

  • Select the trading partner

     

  • Open the draft agreement

     

This is the point where TPM starts to feel much more concrete, because now you are tying the profiles, message structures, mappings, and communication setup together.

5.4.1 B2B Scenarios

Inside the agreement, create the actual B2B scenario.

Recommended example:

  • Name: PARTNER1_ORDERS_ORDERS05_To_850_5010
  • Business Transaction Pattern: One way notification
  • For an outbound scenario, Set your company role to Initiator (For an inbound scenario, Set your company role to Reactor) 

     

     

After creating the transaction:

  1. Select the Sender Communication Channel

     

  2. Select the Sender Interchange and assign the appropriate MIG

     

  3. Maintain the Mapping Process Direct adapter address

     

  4. Configure the Receiver Interchange
    • Select the appropriate MIG
    • Assign the number range
    • Enable functional acknowledgement if required
    • Maintain custom separators if needed

       

  5. Enter the Receiver Communication Channel

Repeat the same process for any additional transactions required for other message types or partners.

Finally, save and activate the agreement.

 

 

In my view, this is the most important part to understand conceptually: the agreement is where TPM becomes executable. Until the agreement is active, the rest of the setup is mostly just preparation.

5.5 Perform End To End Testing

Once the following setups are in place, you are ready for your first end to end test:

  • MIGs are created and activated
  • Standard TPM iFlows are deployed
  • Self company and trading partner profiles are maintained
  • Agreement is configured and activated
  • Custom mapping iFlow is available through ProcessDirect

At this point, you can send a test IDoc from the backend, let Cloud Integration process and transform it into EDI, and verify that the message is successfully routed and delivered to the trading partner.

 

 

 

Final thoughts

When I first started working with Trading Partner Management, it seemed more complex than I expected. There are many setups involved, and at the beginning it is not always obvious what is essential and what can wait. But once I worked through a simple outbound scenario, the overall design started to make much more sense.

What helped me most was thinking about TPM in three parts:

  • message structure with MIGs
  • runtime orchestration with the standard TPM flows
  • business transformation with the custom mapping iFlow

Once those three pieces are clear, TPM becomes much easier to approach.

If you are just getting started, I would recommend building one simple outbound scenario end to end before moving on to multiple partners, multiple message types, or reusable templates. That first working flow gives you a solid foundation for the rest of the setup.

entry.title: Enforcing Package-Level Segregation in a Shared SAP Cloud Integration Tenant
entry.content:
The Problem Nobody Talks About

Shared SAP Integration Suite tenants are more common than SAP's documentation assumes. Whether the reason is cost optimization, organizational structure, or a multi-subsidiary model, many teams end up with developers and operators from different organizations working on the same Cloud Integration tenant.

At some point, someone asks the question:

How do we make sure our developers cannot accidentally touch another organization's integration flows?

You open the SAP Help Portal and review the standard role collections: PI_Integration_Developer, PI_Administrator, PI_Business_Expert, PI_Read_Only. They are well designed and aligned with common personas — but they are tenant-wide by default. Assigning one of them gives the user access across the entire tenant, not just to a specific organization's packages.

That gap is what this article addresses.

Why Standard Role Collections Are Not Enough

SAP's standard role collections answer one question well:

What can this user do?

In a shared tenant, there is a second question that matters just as much:

On which packages can they do it?

Without an answer to the second question, you expose yourself to real risks:

  • A developer from Organization A can edit or redeploy integration flows belonging to Organization B.
  • An operator from Organization A can configure and deploy Organization B artifacts.
  • A tracing user from Organization A can inspect message payloads from Organization B processes, potentially exposing sensitive business data.

The solution is not to replace SAP's authorization model. It is to extend it correctly.

The Architecture: Two Layers of Control

The model combines two mechanisms that work independently but must be used together.

Layer 1 — Role Collections: what the user can do

Custom role collections in SAP BTP define the functional permissions: configure parameters, deploy artifacts, monitor logs, edit integration flows, manage security materials, view payloads. This is standard BTP role management, and it answers the first question.

Layer 2 — Access Policies: which packages those actions apply to

Access Policies in SAP Cloud Integration restrict the scope of the functional permissions to a specific set of integration packages. A user may have permission to configure and deploy — but only for the packages explicitly listed in the corresponding Access Policy.

The link between the two layers is a custom role created from CustomRoleTemplate in BTP. This custom role carries a static attribute (custom_role) whose value must match the Access Policy role name in Cloud Integration exactly. That value is the binding key between both layers.

Both layers are required:

  • Role collections without Access Policies remain tenant-wide.
  • Access Policies without the matching custom role are not enforced.
  • A mismatch in the attribute value — including case differences — breaks the binding silently.

Diagram 1 — Two-Layer Architecture

How the Authorization Check Works

Understanding the enforcement mechanism helps you design and troubleshoot the model correctly.

When a user attempts to act on an integration package — configure, deploy, edit, trace — Cloud Integration performs a two-level check:

Level 1 — Task check Does the user's role collection include the role template required for this action? The functional scopes are evaluated from the user's XSUAA token. If WorkspacePackagesConfigure is absent, access is denied regardless of anything else.

Level 2 — Scope check Does the user have a custom role whose custom_role attribute matches an Access Policy that references this package? The runtime resolves the attribute at call time and compares it against the registered Access Policy role names. If no matching policy covers the target package, access is denied even if the user has the functional role template.

Both checks must pass independently. The practical consequence:

  • Functional role without a matching Access Policy custom role → blocked on every protected package.
  • Custom role without the functional role template → blocked on every action regardless of package.
  • Custom role present, but the target package not listed in the Access Policy → blocked on that specific package.

This is genuine platform-level enforcement, not just UI restrictions.

What You Need to Know Before You Start

These are the behaviors that are easy to miss and hard to diagnose when you get them wrong.

Role collections are scoped per subaccount. A role collection created in DEV does not exist in PRD. Each subaccount where the shared tenant is consumed requires its own set of role collections and custom roles. Access Policies, by contrast, live inside the Cloud Integration tenant and are shared across subaccounts.

Create the role collection before the custom role. The BTP wizard for creating a custom role requires you to assign it to an existing role collection. If the target collection does not exist yet, you cannot complete the wizard. The recommended order is: role collection first, then custom role, then Access Policy.

The attribute value must be confirmed as a tag. When configuring the custom_role attribute in the custom role wizard, typing the value is not enough. You must press Enter to confirm it as a tag. The Next button will not activate until this is done. This is easy to overlook and produces no error message.

PI_Business_Expert is a role collection, not a role template. If you need tracing and payload visibility in a custom role collection, you cannot nest PI_Business_Expert inside it — SAP BTP does not support nesting role collections. The capability you need is the underlying role template AuthGroup_BusinessExpert (it!bXX). And critically: without combining it with your organization's custom role, payload visibility applies across the entire tenant.

Package references are not dynamic. Access Policies do not auto-discover packages. Each integration package must be added explicitly as an artifact reference (Type: Integration Package, Condition: Equals, Value: exact package name). A naming convention helps with governance, but it does not automatically protect new packages — that requires an operational process.

Diagram 2 — Creation Order

Implementing the Pattern

The implementation follows three steps. The SAP Help Portal documents the role templates and Access Policy configuration in detail; the focus here is on the design decisions and sequencing that matter in a shared tenant context.

Step 1 — Define your profiles and their functional scope

Before touching BTP, decide what each organizational profile needs to do. A typical setup for a production environment includes at least:

  • An operator profile — configure parameters, deploy artifacts, monitor logs. No iFlow edit access.
  • A tracing profile — payload visibility and trace activation, kept separate from the operator profile so it can be assigned temporarily during incident investigation and revoked afterward.
  • An administrator profile — full design-time access including iFlow edit, transport, and Access Policy management.

For non-production environments, a developer profile with full design-time capabilities is added in the DEV subaccount only.

Each profile becomes a custom role collection. The functional role templates for each profile are documented in SAP Help — Tasks and Permissions.

Step 2 — Create the custom role from CustomRoleTemplate

In BTP Cockpit, navigate to Security → Roles and search for CustomRoleTemplate under the Cloud Integration runtime application (identifier it!bXX — the exact value is tenant-specific, verify it in your subaccount).

Create a custom role with:

  • A descriptive name (e.g., ORGA_PI_PackageAccess)
  • Attribute: custom_role, Source: Static, Value: same as the role name
  • Assigned to the first role collection you created

Then add this custom role to every additional role collection that must be scoped to the same organization's packages. Any role collection missing the custom role operates without scope restriction.

Step 3 — Create the Access Policy in Cloud Integration

In Integration Suite, navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Manage Security → Access Policies.

Create a policy with a Role Name that matches the custom role attribute value exactly. Then add one artifact reference per integration package owned by the organization:

  • Type: Integration Package
  • Attribute: Name
  • Condition: Equals
  • Value: exact package name as shown in Cloud Integration

No wildcard or regex matching is available for this combination — each package must be listed individually.

Diagram 3 — Shared Tenant Model

Keeping the Model Consistent Over Time

The initial setup is the easy part. The harder part is operational discipline around new packages.

Every time a new integration package is created, it must be explicitly added to the corresponding Access Policy. Until it is, the package is unprotected — any user with the right functional role templates can act on it, regardless of which organization owns it.

A practical governance process:

  1. Enforce a naming convention (e.g., ORGA_*) so package ownership is unambiguous.
  2. Add the package to the Access Policy as part of the package creation checklist — before any iFlow is deployed into it.
  3. No changes to custom roles or role collections are required for new packages.

The naming convention does not enforce protection automatically. It makes the governance process auditable.

Conclusion

The combination of custom role collections and Access Policies gives you genuine package-level segregation in a shared SAP Cloud Integration tenant — enforced at the platform level, not just through UI restrictions.

The model has a deliberate boundary: it covers Cloud Integration (CPI) design-time and runtime access. API Management — API proxies, API products, Developer Hub permissions — has a different authorization model and requires a separate governance approach.

The two things that determine whether this model holds up in practice are not technical: they are the operational process for new packages, and the governance over who holds role collections that include AccessPoliciesEdit. The platform enforces the rules you define. Defining them consistently is your responsibility.

AI disclosure: AI tools were used for language tuning and structure. The technical content, implementation decisions, and validation were performed by the author on a live SAP Integration Suite tenant running Integration Suite 4.7.0 and Cloud Integration Runtime 8.40.25.

References

  • SAP Help — Managing Access Policies (Cloud Foundry)
  • SAP KBA 3223361 — Configure and deploy without edit access in #SAP-Cloud-Integration
  • SAP KBA 3270689 — Creating a customized role for #SAP-Cloud-Integration in Cloud Foundry
  • SAP Blog — Access Policies: Restrict Access to Integration Artifacts in #SAP-Integration-Suite
  • SAP Blog — Access Policies for Integration Packages (increment 2401+) in #SAP-Integration-Suite
  • SAP Blog — Strengthening SAP Cloud Integration Security in #SAP-Integration-Suite

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA - Validated Runtime - Part III
entry.content:
EventSmartKafka is an open-source custom Kafka Consumer Sender Adapter for SAP BTP Integration Suite / SAP Cloud Integration, built with SAP ADK.

This Part III article focuses on the runnable release path: how to install the ESA adapter, import the demo iFlows, understand the security/runtime decisions, and review what was validated under controlled non-production stress tests.

This is an independent solo research project, shared openly with the SAP Integration community. It is not a commercial product, not an SAP-certified adapter, and not a production SLA claim.

“Kafka was never the hardest part. The real challenge was carrying native Kafka TCP through SAP Cloud Connector without exposing the broker, without adding a VPN, and without hiding failures behind metadata timeouts.” — Ricardo Viana, 2026

1. What This Article Covers

Part I introduced the adapter motivation and architecture.

  • SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch — Part I

Part II opened the runtime internals: metadata, endpoint creation, Apache Camel consumer lifecycle, polling loop and offset commit behavior.

  • SAP BTP - CI - Building Open-Source Custom Adapter EventSmartKafka from Zero with SAP ADK — Part II

Part III is the runnable release guide.

QuestionAnswer in this article
Can I install it?Yes. Import the ESA adapter, import the demo package, configure broker/topic/credentials and deploy the iFlows.
Why does it work?The adapter derives Kafka security profiles deterministically and can relay native Kafka TCP through SAP Connectivity SOCKS5 and SAP Cloud Connector.
What was validated?Security profiles, Cloud Connector relay behavior, Avro conversion, recovery behavior and controlled stress execution.

The goal is not to document every Java class. The full operational depth belongs in the GitHub documentation. This blog keeps the path readable.

2. Fast Path — Run It

 

Fast Path
1. Download the .esa from GitHub Releases.
2. Import the EventSmartKafka ESA adapter.
3. Import the demo iFlow package.
4. Configure credentials, Kafka topics and broker address.
5. Deploy the iFlows.

For most users, the pre-built ESA path is the correct path. Building from source is optional.

GitHub Repository

  • https://github.com/rhviana/sap-ci-opensource-adapters

ESA Releases

  • custom-kafka/eventSmartKafka (.esa) releases

Demo iFlows

  • custom-kafka/pkg-adapter-custom
  • custom-kafka/pkg-iflows-sales-otc

Documentation

  • Executive Presentation
  • EventSmartKafka Adapter Configuration and Operations Guide

Optional source build:

git clone https://github.com/rhviana/sap-ci-opensource-adapters.git
cd sap-ci-opensource-adapters
mvn clean install

3. Security Profiles

EventSmartKafka derives the Kafka protocol from two channel decisions:

  • Is authentication enabled?
  • Is TLS enabled?
AuthenticationTLSKafka ProtocolCredentials
NONEOffPLAINTEXTNo
NONEOnSSLNo
SASLOffSASL_PLAINTEXTYes — User Credentials alias
SASLOnSASL_SSLYes — User Credentials alias
Validated consumer-side connectivity and security profiles.

When Authentication = SASL, the adapter supports PLAIN, SCRAM-SHA-256 and SCRAM-SHA-512. When TLS is enabled, trust can come from the JVM default truststore, PEM content, or CPI Trusted Certificate Alias.

The main point is operational: wrong security profile selection can look like a Kafka metadata timeout, TLS handshake failure, authentication failure or broker unreachable. The adapter makes the selection explicit and fails earlier when the channel is inconsistent.

Broker & Platform Compatibility

PlatformValidation Status
Confluent CloudValidated
RedpandaValidated
Aiven for Apache KafkaValidated
Strimzi (Kubernetes)Partially validated
Local Kafka (Docker, ports 19101–19103)Validated — also used for the controlled stress test in Section 7

Across these platforms, the following channel combinations were exercised end-to-end: PLAINTEXT (no auth, no TLS), SSL (TLS only, no auth), SASL_PLAINTEXT with PLAIN, and SASL_SSL with SCRAM-SHA-256 and SCRAM-SHA-512.

4. Cloud Connector SOCKS5 Relay — Kafka Without Public Exposure

Apache Kafka does not speak HTTP. It uses native binary TCP. Standard HTTP-style Cloud Connector access is not enough for native Kafka.

EventSmartKafka uses a local TCP relay inside the CPI worker and forwards Kafka TCP traffic through SAP Connectivity SOCKS5 and SAP Cloud Connector.

SAP CPI Worker
└── EventSmartKafka Sender Adapter
   └── Apache Kafka Java Client
      └── connects to 127.0.0.1:<brokerPort>
        └── SdiaCloudConnectorTcpTunnel
          └── SAP Connectivity SOCKS5 proxy
            └── SAP Cloud Connector
              └── On-premise Kafka broker

Result: Kafka remains behind the corporate network boundary. No public Kafka endpoint and no VPN are required for this validation path.

Cloud Connector TCP mappings must align with the Kafka broker listener and advertised listener strategy.

Two custom classes support this path:

ClassResponsibility
SdiaCloudConnectorTcpProbeValidates the SOCKS5 CONNECT path before Kafka client initialization.
SdiaCloudConnectorTcpTunnelBinds a local relay port and forwards Kafka TCP streams through SAP Connectivity SOCKS5.

Kafka opens multiple TCP connections internally: metadata, fetch, heartbeat and offset commit. The relay must handle those streams transparently.

A note on authorship. Both SdiaCloudConnectorTcpProbe and SdiaCloudConnectorTcpTunnel were written from scratch for this project — there is no upstream library doing the SOCKS5-over-Cloud-Connector relay work; the TCP framing, the CONNECT handshake and the stream forwarding logic are original.

Each source file carries an attribution header identifying the author and the Apache 2.0 license terms. They are shared freely with the SAP Integration community — fork them, extend them, build on top of them — the only ask is to leave that header intact, the same way this project credits the open-source libraries it depends on.

5. Advertised Listeners: The Most Common Trap

Kafka bootstrap is only the first connection. After bootstrap, the broker returns metadata containing advertised broker addresses. The Kafka client then reconnects to those advertised addresses.

Advertised listener pointing to an unreachable internal hostname — the client reconnects after metadata and stalls, even though the initial bootstrap succeeded.

For Cloud Connector relay scenarios, advertised listeners must point back to relay-compatible addresses. If Kafka advertises an internal hostname that CPI cannot resolve or reach, the result can look like a metadata timeout even when Kafka and Cloud Connector are both running.

This is Kafka metadata behavior. It does not automatically mean that Avro, Schema Registry, SAP Cloud Connector, or the adapter runtime is broken.

6. Runtime Features Kept in Scope

Part III keeps the runtime feature explanation short. The detailed behavior is documented in the repository.

FeatureSummary
Offset resetEARLIEST, LATEST and NONE.
Error handlingStop on Error, Skip Failed Message and Retry Failed Message.
TIMESEEKReplay from timestamp without changing consumer group ID.
Avro conversionAvro to JSON/XML, Confluent Magic Byte, fixed schema ID and raw passthrough.

These features are important, but they are not the main story of this article. The main story is that native Kafka consumption can run through the Cloud Connector SOCKS5 path without exposing the broker.

Earlist - Latest - None:

 

Seek by timestamp:

One of the most powerfull features of this custom Kafka adapter channel, you are able to recover any messages that was comitt already based on the timestamp.

Error Handling — The Offset Controller (Retry / Skip / Stop):

Validating broker connectivity and security profiles is only half the problem. The other half is what happens after a message is polled: what if it fails Avro conversion? What if it's a poison pill that will fail forever? EventSmartKafka centralizes that decision in a single component — the Offset Controller.

 

The Offset Controller governs what happens after polling: payload validation, Avro conversion, and routing through Retry, Skip or Stop — before any offset is committed.

The flow: poll from Kafka → payload validation → Avro conversion. If conversion succeeds, the record goes to the CPI pipeline and the offset is committed only after that processing succeeds. If conversion fails, it never reaches the commit path directly — it's routed to the Offset Strategy Engine instead:

StragegyBehavior
RetryRe-polls the same offset, up to a configurable max attempt count
SkipLogs and audits the bad record, then advances to the next offset
StopHalts consumption and raises an alert for manual intervention

Only successful (or explicitly skipped) records reach the Offset Commit Controller, which centralizes commit logic so no message is ever marked as consumed before it's actually been processed. Records that exhaust retries or are explicitly skipped can be routed to a dead-letter / error channel for later analysis and reprocessing.

This is the same mechanism behind the "Error handling" row above — Stop on Error, Skip Failed Message and Retry Failed Message aren't just config flags, they're entry points into this engine.

Why it matters: prevents silent data loss, handles poison pills safely, gives full control over offset behavior, ensures data consistency, and is built for enterprise-grade integration.

Full retry-backoff and dead-letter configuration is documented in docs/OFFSETS_ERROR_HANDLING_TIMESEEK.md.

7. Controlled Stress Validation

Before shipping an adapter, one message is not enough. The relevant question is what happens when multiple runtime paths are active at the same time.

The first parallel stress validation executed:

  • multiple security profiles;
  • multiple Cloud Connector TCP tunnels;
  • Avro conversion;
  • raw passthrough;
  • offset-safe consumption;
  • parallel iFlows.
MetricValue
Messages1.5M+
Completed1.5M+
Failed0
Retries0

This was a controlled non-production validation using local/controlled Kafka and SAP BTP Trial resources. It proves design behavior under that test harness. It does not claim SAP certification, commercial SLA, 24-hour endurance soak, real WAN latency simulation, or independent benchmark certification.

Scope of this stress run. The 1.5M+ message run was executed against local Kafka specifically, not against the cloud-managed brokers listed in Section 3 or an on-premise broker reached through SAP Cloud Connector. As an independent solo researcher, sustained high-volume traffic against managed cloud Kafka or production-grade on-premise infrastructure was outside the available budget for this validation round — that level of testing is left for a future, sponsored or community-supported cycle.

The local Kafka instance exposed three parallel SOCKS5 tunnels (ports 19101, 19102 and 19103), each carrying a different authentication mode at the same time — for example:

  • SASL_SSL
  • SCRAM-SHA-512 on one tunnel,
  • SASL_PLAINTEXT on another, and PLAINTEXT on the third — to validate that security profile derivation holds under concurrent, mixed-mode traffic. Avro payload sizes of 53 KB, 1 MB and 2 MB were included in the same run to validate conversion behavior across message sizes, not just message count.

 

Video of adapter, configuration, and stress tests runtime - Recored video:

8. Operational Note — Local Relay Port Locked After Hot Deploy

During intensive deploy/redeploy cycles, especially when iFlows reuse the same on-premise TCP relay port, an older CPI/OSGi adapter instance may temporarily keep a local relay port open.

Visible symptom:

Address already in use

Observed behavior:

Restarting SAP Cloud Connector does not release the port.
Changing only the local relay port makes the adapter work immediately.

That indicates the port is held on the CPI/JVM side, not by Kafka and not by SAP Cloud Connector.

Local TCP relay port lock observed after intensive CPI deploy/redeploy cycles. Kafka and SAP Cloud Connector can remain healthy while the CPI-side local relay port is still held by an older runtime instance.

Recommended recovery sequence:

  1. Deploy the new EventSmartKafka ESA.
  2. Redeploy every iFlow/channel using EventSmartKafka.
  3. Wait for channels to restart and recreate endpoints.
  4. Test the same relay ports again.
  5. Change ports only if Address already in use persists after the full redeploy cycle.

This is an adapter lifecycle recovery procedure after ESA or iFlow redeploy cycles. It is not a Kafka issue and not a Cloud Connector issue.

9. Architectural Context — SDIA, EDCP and ODCP

EventSmartKafka was validated inside a broader SDIA model, but the adapter can be understood independently.

In that context:

  • EDCP governs event, messaging and streaming topology by domain.
  • ODCP governs orchestration artifacts, packages, iFlows and credentials by domain.

The practical principle is simple: Kafka topics, CPI iFlows, credentials and runtime outcomes should remain aligned to the same business domain boundary.

10. What Is Not in v1.0

FeatureStatus
OAuth 2.0 / OAUTHBEARERRoadmap
mTLS client certificate authenticationRoadmap
Protobuf conversionRoadmap
Producer / Receiver roleRoadmap
Dead-letter topic for skipped messagesRoadmap
Enterprise stress certificationRequires independent validation
SLA or commercial supportNot a commercial product

EventSmartKafka is an open-source community technical project. Production use requires validation by the adopting organization.

entry.title: Integration between S/4HANA Cloud and Integration Suite with SOAP Outbound service using Output Type
entry.content:
In this blog post we will discuss about Integration between S/4 HANA Cloud to external system via Integration Suite using Output Type as the trigger from S/4 HANA Side. This can be achieved provided there is a standard Outbound service available within any standard SAP Communication scenario. This pattern can also be considered as a good alternative for EDA when there are no standard events available for the business object and in those cases, it can be used instead of going for custom events. The outbound service will use SOAP technology so every time you trigger you will get a XML payload with full data as per API specification.

Advantage – It gives you the full data every time so no need for additional OData calls to S/4 HANA to fetch additional details.

Disadvantage – You do not get delta changes, but you will get the full data every time you trigger. So you can control it from middleware mapping according to your business need.

To demonstrate this use-case today I am using S/4 HANA Public Cloud. I will write a separate blog for S/4 HANA Private Cloud. To configure the triggering using Output Type from S/4 HANA Public cloud we will need to use five standard applications in S/4 HANA.

  1. Maintain Communication Users
  2. Communication Systems
  3. Communication Arrangements
  4. Output Parameter Determination
  5. Assign Recipients to Users (Optional for monitoring purpose)

Step 1 – Create a basic Integration flow with SOAP Sender adapter and deploy it.

In this blog we are only focusing on the part of integration from S/4 HANA Cloud to Integration Suite. The Output Type triggering will be done using a SOAP Outbound service which will eventually send an XML payload. Based on the technology used(API/SFTP) on target side it can be converted or sent as it is after evaluating the capability of the target system to interpret an XML file.

 

Copy the deployed URL and SOAP Address part to be used later.

Step 2 – Activating Communication

We will be using standard communication scenario SAP_COM_0223 to demonstrate the use case here. However, there are other communication scenarios too which has outbound SOAP services available. SAP_COM_0223 has three outbound services, here we will trigger the Outbound delivery.

I have used SAP Business Accelerator Hub to identify my required API for my requirement. API - https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-CE_CO_EDI_DELIVERY_REQUEST_V2_OUT-v2/overview

We need to create a communication arrangement for this standard communication scenario. How to create communication arrangements and its related communication system and communication user that I have covered in one of my previous blogs - API Consumption - S/4 HANA Cloud,Public Edition a... - SAP Community . Also, there are multiple other blogs available in SAP community on this topic.

The hierarchy is – Communication User -> Communication System -> Communication Arrangement

*Additional step for this scenario you have to add a Outbound User  in communication system as in this case our communication direction is S/4 HANA -> External(outgoing)

Also "Inbound only" checkbox should be unchecked in this case.

Most important you have to add the host of your Integration Suite in the field “Host Name” as your S/4 HANA system is communicating with Integration Suite via this communication system.

Once we have created the arrangement, we need to configure the Integration flow URL components in the two fields highlighted in the below screenshot

In the “Path” provide the portion of your deployed URL which is highlighted in the below screenshot

The “Service URL” part is non editable and it is supposed to populated automatically from your assigned communication system when you configure the Host Name in your communication system as shown above

Step 3 – Configuring Output Parameter

Configure the Output Type in the app “Output Parameter Determination”

Open the app –

Configure the standard output type DELIVERY_NOTE for Determination Step = "Output Type"  for “EDI” .

Step 4 – Maintain BP in Communication System

Maintain the business partners in communication system. This is an important step as without this your Output does not gets triggered. The BP for which you are intending to create your Sales Order, and corresponding Outbound delivery needs to be maintained in this section.

Go to “Communication System” app, open the communication system that you are using for the particular communication arrangement and add the BPs under this tab.

Test your Interface using an existing Outbound Delivery or creating a new one.

Here I have triggered output manually but automatic triggering to be configured with appropriate conditions from “Output Parameter Determination”

Go to app – “Change Outbound Delivery”/VL02N and open the delivery number for which you want to trigger the output type.

Go to path –

Menu -> Extras -> Delivery Output -> Output Control

Click on “Add Output” and then click on “Send Output”

Once output shows as “Completed” you can check the message in your Integration flow if it has reached. You can check your Iflow monitoring tab for message received in your Integration Suite–

So once you have the XML payload in your Integration flow, you can do necessary conversion/mapping or send as it is to target using API or SFTP whichever capability your target system supports.

**Additional tips -  For monitoring you can create a recipient for this interface from the application – Assign Recipients to Users with names space and recipient name as below -

You can monitor SOAP outbound messages from the applications like –

Message Monitoring for Integration Experts

Message Monitoring

entry.title: Using the AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite to Generate Integration Incident Summaries
entry.content:
Introduction

When an integration fails, support teams often need to interpret technical error messages before they can understand the root cause and decide on the next action. For experienced integration developers, this may be straightforward, but for first-line support or business users, the message can be difficult to translate into something actionable.

In this mini-blog, a simple proof of concept shows how SAP Integration Suite and a Large Language Model (LLM) can transform a raw integration error payload into a structured incident summary by using the built-in AI Adapter in Cloud Integration.

For adapter capabilities, configuration details, and supported variants, refer to SAP Help and existing other SAP blog post by Shashank_K.

Scenario

In real integration scenarios, an incident often contains only a few technical fields, such as:

  • MPL ID.
  • Timestamp.
  • Endpoint.
  • Error message.

Instead of manually interpreting these values every time, the flow sends the incident details to an LLM and asks it to generate a structured summary containing:

  • Incident summary.
  • Root cause.
  • Severity.
  • Recommendation.

This helps reduce analysis time and makes incident triage easier for support teams.

Solution Overview

The integration flow receives the incident payload through an HTTP endpoint and forwards the content to an LLM using the AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite. The AI Adapter for SAP Integration Suite facilitates and accelerates connectivity to Generative AI Hub (SAP AI Core) and other AI providers and is available in AICore-GenAIHub and Custom-OpenAI-REST variants. The LLM returns a structured incident summary that can be displayed, logged, or forwarded to another support process.

Client -> Cloud Integration -> AI Adapter -> LLM (SAP AI Core - GenAI Hub) -> Structured Incident Summary

Building the Integration Flow

Prerequisites:
This mini-blog assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite and have already provisioned SAP AI Core with GenAI Hub enabled. If you are new to SAP AI Core, you can start with the SAP Developers mission to set up SAP AI Core on SAP Business Technology Platform and then explore concepts such as orchestration and the foundation models that make up SAP AI Core: Set Up SAP AI Core in SAP BTP.

The Integration Flow in my demo scenario contains the following steps:

  • HTTPS Sender Adapter.
  • JSON to XML Converter.
  • Content Modifier.
  • Request-Reply step.
  • AI Adapter.
  • JSON to XML Converter.
  • Content Modifier.
  • HTTPS Response.

Here is how my Integration Flow looks like. The JSON to XML Converter step transforms the incoming JSON payload into XML format so it can be processed more easily by downstream steps in the iFlow, since the payload is sent as JSON from Bruno, a REST client tool.

Create the exchange properties below to map the values that will be used later in the message flow between Request Reply 1 and the Receiver.

On Message Flow between Request Reply 1 to Receiver, fill-in the connection details (you can get the details from SAP AI Core > Service Key).

Before filling the OAuth2 Client Credentials field, first create the OAuth2 Client Credentials in Monitor > Integrations and APIs > Manage Security Material. Here are example configurations.

In my demo scenario, for the Processing details configuration, I'm using the following:
 

Operation

Orchestration - Completion

Orchestration - Deployment ID

$header.deployment_id

AI Resource Group

Default

Request Payload Source

UI Configurable

Model

Gemini-2.5-flash

Role

Assistant

Input

You are an SAP Integration Suite support assistant. Analyze ONLY the provided incident information (Timestamp=${property.timestamp}, Endpoint=${property.endpoint}, Error Message=${property.errorMessage}) and return ONLY: Summary, Root Cause, Severity (Low|Medium|High), Recommendation. Max 100 words. No explanations, no markdown, no placeholders, no extra text.

Request Headers

*

As I don't want to hard-code the Orchestration - Deployment ID, so I'm passing it in my HTTP headers with key name deployment_id.

Refer to this SAP Help documentation page, if you need to do variant of configuration such as using Exchange Body as Request Payload Source allows you to specify required structure and values.
Note: the input content should be 1 liner, otherwise you will get an error upon deployment.

Map the result into aiResult in Exchange Property:

Finally in Message Body return the following JSON as final output:

Testing the Flow

You can test the flow using Bruno or any REST client tool (i.e. Postman or cUrl) by sending the incident payload to the HTTP endpoint.

Input payload:

{
	  "mplId": "AGo046SjNI9VZ4l",
	  "timestamp": "2026-06-22T10:00:00Z",
	  "endpoint": "/api/orders",
	  "errorMessage": "OAuth token validation failed"
}

Response payload:
 

{
	"incidentAnalysis": "Summary: API calls to /api/orders are failing due to OAuth token validation failure.
	Root Cause: Invalid or expired OAuth token used for authentication to the /api/orders endpoint.
	Severity: Medium
	Recommendation: Verify current OAuth token validity, expiration, and scopes. Regenerate or refresh the token as needed."
}

Example from Bruno:

Key Takeaways

SAP Integration Suite can do more than move data between systems — with the AI Adapter, it becomes easier to integrate with an LLM and turn raw technical errors into structured summaries. This helps support teams understand failures faster, identify likely root causes, and take the right next action.
